body.text: DEMOCRATIZING THE IMAGINATION OF THE FUTURE
We Create
We cannot create what we cannot imagine.
Lucille Clifton, poet
FOREWORD
The future is not fixed.
Anticipating, creating, and influencing possible tomorrows starts whenever someone asks “What could happen?” and, more importantly, “What do we want to make happen?”

Futuring is not about trying to predict what will happen. It’s about observing our present through a lens of what’s possible. It’s also about dreaming alternative trajectories. It’s about recognizing that the stories about tomorrow that we are telling today can become the blueprints for possible tomorrows.
It’s about expanding who gets to imagine the future.

When we engage more people from more places and more walks of life to imagine futures together, we open ourselves up to possibilities that would never emerge from current dominant perspectives becoming visions that could transform society.
The future is not a destination we’re traveling toward—we create it together, every day, with every choice, every story of tomorrow we tell, every possibility we dream.
We think the world is ready for more equitable futures.
A NOTE OF WELCOME

Seven years ago, I was having coffee with a new colleague at Robert Wood Johnson Foundation (RWJF). I wanted to tap her network of movement leaders as I looked to fill a program officer position on the Ideas for an Equitable Future (aka “Futures”) team I direct. This team, I explained, is tasked with anticipating, adapting to, and influencing the future toward a north star of health equity. We fund projects—including the work of professional “futurists”—to help spot signals of change and emerging trends and understand how those larger shifts in society might impact RWJF’s work toward a future where health is no longer a privilege but a right.

I told her I was looking for someone curious, creative and… “you know, someone who spends their time thinking about the future.” She nodded and then said something that made me stop and think: Lori, to be honest, I don’t think many in my network feel they have the time to think about the future in that way. They’re focused on current crises. For them, putting out fires and making immediate progress is more urgent than anticipating and envisioning the future.

I let her words sink in. They resonated, not just as sound insight for someone seeking to build a robust team, but also as something a team focused on the future needs to consider in the context of our grantmaking—that while everyone has the ability to imagine and contemplate the future, few leaders may have the access to the time, space, and support to engage in futuring. I shared this hunch with my team.

As the pandemic erupted and the future became more difficult—and important—to anticipate, my team and I explored how to enable more people to engage in futuring to advance health equity. We heard, anecdotally, that many organizations working toward social justice did not have the resources to work with a futurist or the time to engage in futuring. Even if they did, finding futurist facilitators with expertise or personal experience related to their organizational goals was challenging.

We began to provide support to leaders and organizations that would allow them to bring their futuring practices to community-serving organizations, thereby helping those organizations anticipate and imagine the equitable futures they were advancing toward.

We don’t need to reimagine the world. We need to imagine the world. With reimagination we inherit and try to build in the confines of our knowledge and existence. In BIPOC communities… often that’s trying to fix something that was never intended for us to flourish in the first place.
David Bickham

As we expanded the number and range of futuring activities we funded, I saw social workers in Oregon, members of the Future of Nursing committee in Washington D.C., youth in Jackson, Mississippi, and a group of rural leaders across the country open their eyes wide as they began to understand the implications of emerging trends—shifts in society that have the potential to help or hinder wellbeing for all. I saw energy rise as co-created visions revealed inspiring futures worth fighting for and surfaced steps these organizations could take now to advance into those futures. I began to see how important it was for all organizations and communities to have the time, space, and tools to anticipate, adapt to, and influence the future.

One day on a call with Kamal Sinclair—who, at the time, was directing the Guild of Future Architects, one of the organizations we funded—I heard her talk about their work as “democratizing the imagination of the future.” This phrase really struck me. I realized this was the strategy that had been revealing itself for the past two years.

As the team’s work in what we were now calling “democratizing the imagination of the future opportunity space” progressed, we supported more futuring leaders whose personal experience with structural racism informed their approaches. Several of these projects focused on social justice, art, community organizing, and imagining liberatory futures.

As a learning team, we supported futuring practitioners who experimented with many different approaches. Grantees developed, adapted, and applied new and established methods, tools, and strategies. They engaged communities with games, books, and dance parties in their workshops. Not only did these futuring activities enhance the effectiveness of community-led organizations, they also helped staff across the Foundation experiment with sensemaking and trend-sensing.

This report synthesizes what the Futures team at RWJF has learned about what is needed to promote futuring that advances equity for the fields of public health, racial justice, philanthropy, and foresight, and why and how that matters.

And it introduces you to 13 brilliant futuring practitioners and several other grantees who are shaping our collective visions of more equitable futures. We think their words, advice, and work will inspire you to look to their perspectives, tools, and methods for insight as you consider your strategies for impact.

What was an “aha” moment to me in 2019—that an important way to elevate inclusive equitable futures is to support the conditions for community members to take time to imagine what is possible—should not be groundbreaking to you in a post-2020 world. Presently, many in our country recognize that systemic racism underpins inequity. Futuring is also having a moment in the philanthropic community and beyond. There is no better time to dream up new systems resilient to the inequities than today—as the status quo is being so disrupted by political, cultural, and technological shifts.

What I hope will be YOUR “aha moment” is that there is something you can and must do today to create these conditions for change. Once we understand we are living out the imaginations of those who came before us, we owe it to future generations to have the people who best understand the challenges of structural racism and the potential of health equity leading the way.
I look forward to hearing what you do to influence the future!
Lori Melichar
Senior Director, Ideas for an Equitable Future team, RWJF
ABOUT THIS REPORT
The stories we tell ourselves about how the future will unfold affect what we do—or don’t do—today.

They shape strategy. They drive action. Whose narratives are we listening to? Whose assumptions about what is possible limit or expand how we are dreaming up our tomorrows, our neighbor’s tomorrows, our field’s tomorrows?

In this report, we make the case that to get to healthier tomorrows, we need to resource more people, communities, and organizations today with the time, space, and expertise to imagine futures beyond the constraints of oppressive dominant narratives and perceived limits of what is possible.

After seven years of grantmaking that explored what it means to democratize the imagination of the future, we stepped back and reflected on the body of work. We did this to inform our future funding strategies, and to surface and share learning that might be helpful to our grantees. As we began to understand the various ways a set of RWJF grantees have been futuring, we soon realized we were gathering a rich parcel of insights about how these practices benefit individuals, communities, and the fields of futuring, philanthropy, and racial justice and health equity.
Our Hope
By sharing what we and our grantees have learned about the need to democratize the imagination of the future, we hope to:

items.text: Inspire our philanthropic peers to incorporate futures thinking practices into their own work; support organizations in this pursuit by providing them with time, money, and space to do the same; and fund practitioners with a diverse set of experiences across the field who are advancing tools and methods of speculative visioning

Inspire leaders in racial justice and health equity to explore how such approaches might help them to interrogate their strategies, engage their communities, cultivate hope, prepare for the future, and develop bold interventions and

body.text: compelling narratives

◊ Inspire people, movements, and organizations across the social sector to take up the charge to spend time anticipating the impact of emerging trends, conceive of the futures they want to inhabit, build resilience through creativity, and discover new ways to work with others to bring those futures to life

Read on to learn about and from different approaches to futuring, and join us as we question our terms, explore the unique value of these practices, and discover links to tools and methods to go deeper.
Our Four Starting Questions
As we set out to write this report, we started with four questions.
◊ What has RWJF learned about the value of funding a set of grantees who use futuring and speculative visioning that informs work to advance health equity and counter structural racism?

items.text: What role can futures thinking and speculative visioning play in getting to a future where health is not a privilege, but a right?
What is the impact and value of supporting this work to grantees, organizations, communities, and the field?

- Whatisneededtoenablemorepeopleand organizations—inside and outside of the fields of philanthropy and health—to understand the value of and use various methods of futuring and speculative visioning as they consider their own role in shaping healthy futures?

body.text: It’s not a surprise that we ended with many more questions— some answered, some not—which we will speak to throughout this report.
How We Wrote It

Robert Wood Johnson Foundation’s Ideas for an Equitable Future team (aka the “Futures” team) commissioned Jessica Clark—founder and executive director of strategy and foresight firm Dot Connector Studio, and the Foundation’s previous Futurist in Residence—to take a deep dive into insights from leaders of a set of grant-funded projects focused on futuring, innovation, and emergent approaches to social change.

Clark was a foresight strategist and author who spent the past decade working hand-in-hand with many of the country’s largest foundations to understand how society, media, and technology are evolving—and how to design programs and productions to meet the moment. She spent four years embedded in the Futures team as RWJF’s Futurist in Residence, a consultant, and a participant-observer.

Dot Connector Studio’s research associate Jenna Rines joined Clark to conduct one-on-one interviews with leaders affiliated with current and past RWJF grants. Dot Connector Studio’s team also conducted field and desk research related to foresight, strategic visioning, social change, racial equity, and philanthropic practice. Then, they invited interviewees and other grantees to group discussions to elicit feedback on the research. Their analysis was informed by numerous conversations with RWJF’s Lori Melichar, senior director, and Jody Struve, senior communications officer, as well as conversations with the larger Futures team.

We also invited philanthropic peers inside and outside of the Foundation to weigh in on how democratizing the imagination of the future might be relevant to their work. Many thanks to Alicia Bell of Borealis Philanthropy, David Hsu of Omidyar Network, and Maya Ingram of Democracy Funders Network for their insights. Many thanks to the Dot Connector Studio team who helped this project come to fruition, including Katie Donnelly, Collin Riggins, and Nina Spensley. Thank you to Ghost Note Agency who designed this report. Their director of strategy, Sarah Duarte, was also a key thought partner.

As a result of this highly collaborative and iterative sensemaking process, the insights in this report represent dialogue with the interviewees, and many months of synthesis and revision. In the end, the core research team involved Clark, Melichar, Struve, and Rines. This report reflects the points of view and learning of the core research team, and through direct quotes, the points of view and learning of the interviewed grantees.
How to Read It
The path to the future is not linear. And your path through this report does not need to be either.
After you meet the interviewees in the following pages, stop off at Choose Your Next Chapter and navigate to what’s most resonant for you. We hope that the more you read, the deeper you want to go!
MEET THE INTERVIEWEES
Throughout the report, we share and celebrate the experiences, observations, and wisdom of

children.text: 17 current and past RWJF grantees

body.text: whose work offers perspective on ways to democratize the imagination of the future. The interviewees hail from a variety of disciplines and practices—they are researchers, artists, organizers, social workers, inventors, and more—and many of them also work in novel and interdisciplinary ways.
Clark interviewed a core set of grantees who identified futuring, speculative fiction, or foresight as central to their practice or funded projects.
David Bickham
Award-winning author and poet, founder and chief futurist at Bickham MetaFutures, LLC

Bickham co-led an RWJF grant (2023–2025) to Jackson Medical Mall Foundation to make futurism and creativity an essential component in realizing health equity by codifying and disseminating Bickham’s Prophetic Innovation Consortium framework. This framework reimagines corporate, Western, and some Afrofuturistic paradigms to establish a “new world” of strategic foresight informed by the African American and African Diaspora and universally progressive causes. By strengthening the futuring muscles of leaders, systems, and institutions, this project explores how futurism studies can support institutional, systemic, and policy-related change, and how knowledge can be made accessible and move beyond the few current practitioners.
Lonny J Avi Brooks, PhD
Professor in strategic communications and foresight at California State University, East Bay, co-founder AfroRithm Futures Group, co-producer Afrofuturist Podcast

With support from an RWJF grant (2022– 2025) to Oakland’s Museum of Children’s Art (MOCHA), Brooks co-led, with Nina Woodruff, a project at MOCHA’s Community Futures School to prepare students aged 14–17 to be resourceful and contributing inhabitants of the future. Through community-building, futures literacy, and arts-centered practices, teens “investigate[d], reimagine[d], and identif[ied] solutions that build a more anti-racist, healing-centered, and inclusive Oakland in 2045.” Across the city, students showcased their visions of the future— futures where people like them thrive.
Creston M. Burse, MBA, PhD
Strategic foresight practitioner, technology agent, founder and chief strategy officer at Bemae Group

Burse played a key role in an RWJF grant (2020–2022) to the Jackson Medical Mall Foundation to promote the success of Jackson, Mississippi, public school students as innovators, futurists, and leaders through the Young Futurists Project. As the project’s Foresight Instructor, Burse helped integrate a health and futurism lens into its STEAM education program. The Young Futurists Projects sought to nurture young innovators in a health innovation culture and build capacity for young innovators and leaders to address current, emerging, and undiscovered issues related to health equity.
adrienne maree brown
Emergent Strategist, New York Timesbestselling author, social justice facilitator

With her network of collaborators, brown “nurtures Emergent Strategy, Pleasure Activism, Radical Imagination, and Loving Correction as ideas and practices for transformation.” She co-led an RWJF grant (2021-2023) to build the capacity of the Emergent Strategy Ideation Institute (ESII). Responding to COVID-19, economic instability, anti-Black racism, white supremacy, and patriarchal violence, ESII worked to seed and proliferate experiments with Emergent Strategy to address the lack of cohesiveness and other challenges facing the social justice movement. She was joined in her interview by her collaborator, Caitlin Breedlove.
Lafayette Cruise, MCP
Urban planner, futurist, adjunct professor at Rhode Island School of Design

Cruise leverages the “radical imagination and world-building capabilities of speculative fiction and the multidisciplinary, strategic implementation tools of urban planning in order to imagine, plan, and build a more just, liberating and sustainable future.” He was a founding member of the Regenerative Leadership Team of the Guild of Future Architects (GoFA), which received an RWJF grant (2021–2022) to scale up and equip a diverse collaborative of futurists embedded in communities with the resources and relationships to imagine, prototype, and realize just and inclusive futures.
Marina Gorbis, MPP
Executive director of the Institute for the Future (IFTF), author of The Nature of the Future: Dispatches from the Socialstructed World

IFTF has received a number of RWJF grants and RWJF has been a member of IFTF’s Vantage Program in past years. As a Vantage member (2022–2023), RWJF participated in IFTF’s Forecast Summit to examine evolving values about place and sustainable, equitable growth. An earlier membership (2020–2021) enabled RWJF to work with IFTF to identify emerging trends, and RWJF supported the development of the IFTF Future Factors platform to track signals of the future and connect futurists in a network. IFTF also received an RWJF grant (2021–2023) to map types of equitable enterprises and explore how they promote opportunity, wealth, security, and health for diverse workers and their families.
Karen Lord, MPhil, PhD, MS
Award-winning Barbadian author, editor of speculative and sociological fiction

Lord’s recent novel, The Blue, Beautiful World, was longlisted for the 2024 Women’s Prize for Fiction. She is co-editor of We Will Rise Again: Speculative Stories and Essays on Protest, Resistance, and Hope (2025). Lord’s story, “The Plague Doctors,” published in RWJF’s book of fiction, Take Us to a Better Place: Stories (2000), was selected for The Best American Science Fiction and Fantasy 2021. Lord served as RWJF’s Speculative Fiction Writer in Residence from 2023–2024. In this capacity, she explored the potential of speculative fiction as a cognitive tool to help RWJF staff imagine and chart paths to better, more equitable futures using dialogue and original fiction informed and inspired by sitting in on meetings, and workshops.
Trista Harris, MPP
Philanthropic futurist, professional speaker, founder and president at FutureGood

Harris served as RWJF’s inaugural Futurist in Residence from 2019–2021. With support from an RWJF grant (2021–2022) to FutureGood, Harris’ Field Trips to the Future program engaged a cohort of 30 rural and Native Nation leaders to strengthen the influence of future-oriented thinking in their work. Over the course of a year, the cohort discovered skills as future-focused leaders, explored resources and tools, and built relationships with a diverse set of professional futurists and each other as they advanced equitable rural development and community conditions for wellbeing.
Julia Mossbridge, MA, PhD
Futures shaper, author, cognitive neuroscientist, founder of The Institute for Love and Time (TILT), member of the Alfred Lee Loomis Innovation Council at the Stimson Center

With support from RWJF (2022–2023) to TILT, Mossbridge led a project to develop Time Machine, a “time-travel narrative” cross-platform web application that improves users’ future orientation—or the ability to habitually plan for a positive future. Designed by and for under-resourced people, it has been shown to improve overall wellbeing (as well as other measures related to wellbeing), especially for those who have survived trauma. A second RWJF grant (2024–2025) helped enhance the usability of and educate target audiences about the Time Machine app.
Laura Nissen, MSW, PhD
Disruptive foresight practitioner, former dean and professor emeritus at Portland University, School of Social Work, research fellow at Institute for the Future (IFTF)

Nissen led work on several RWJF grants (2022–2024) to bring social work practitioners into the world of foresight through creation of the Social Work Health Futures Lab and the 2024 Social Work Health Conference from the Future, which explored how foresight and futures thinking might influence the way that social work considers its work related to health and the social determinants of health. Before the Lab, the social work profession had only had cursory engagement with futures thinking. The goal of the conference was to increase the readiness and agility of social workers to meet the challenges of a rapidly changing future and disseminate learning and work from the Lab.
C. Brandon Ogbunu, PhD
Computational biologist, writer, assistant professor at Yale University Department of Ecology and Evolutionary Biology, professor at Sante Fe Institute

Ogbunu leads an RWJF grant (2023–2026) to explore cultural objects as model systems for the study of social inequalities. The goal of this project is to use the products of human culture—relics, institutions, and movements— to explore new research methods, data, and tools that can provide for a more modern understanding of social inequalities and create formal model systems to understand their dynamics. The project outlines three types of cultural relics, and proposes a means through which they can be used as models for examining and understanding the dynamics of social inequalities: 1) speculative fiction as an object for understanding inequities of the present and future; 2) sports as an institution to examine the dynamics of social inequalities; and 3) hip-hop culture as a source of public health data and messaging.
Aisha Shillingford, MSW, MBA
Artist, worldbuilder, experiential futurist, artistic director of Intelligent Mischief

Shillingford co-led, with Terry Marshall, an RWJF-funded project (2024–2025) to implement the Black Imagination Lab which uses the lens of reparations for Black people to imagine and build equitable futures. This research, design, and development program brought together a cohort of multi-sector leaders (arts, media, organizing, philanthropy, policy, governance, etc.) within and representing poor and working-class communities of color, and their allies, to build their imaginal capacity. Together they collectively imagined, designed, and developed worlds, stories, and experiences that reflected their visions of transformative futures that address the most intractable issues impacting the health and wellbeing of Black communities. They created reproducible tools and workbooks and story ideas that can animate and make this future world understandable. A “Museum of Repair” experience launched in 2025 presents a collection of artifacts, experiences, stories, and content to the general public.
Kamal Sinclair, MBA
Artist, producer, senior director of digital innovation at The Music Center in Los Angeles

Sinclair supports artists, institutions, and communities working at the convergence of art, media, culture, and technology. She previously served as executive director of Guild of Future Architects (GoFA) which received an RWJF general operating grant (2021-2022). Under her leadership, in the context of recovery and progress sparked by COVID-19, the national centering of racial justice, and unprecedented threats to democracy, GoFA helped people navigate disruptions at institutional, community, and national levels through practices of co-creation and complex inclusive design.

children.text: To round out the findings for this report, Clark interviewed additional RWJF grantees who have explored futuring but whose RWJFfunded work did not focus on it.

body.text: Sasha Costanza-Chock, PhD
Researcher, designer, author, head of research at OneProject.org, faculty associate at Berkman-Klein Center for Internet & Society

Costanza-Chock co-led, with Denise Shanté Brown, an RWJFfunded project (2022–2024) to strengthen the Design Justice Network and deepen knowledge and understanding of equitable access to systems and structures of care. The project sought to improve equitable access to Care Pods—small groups to engage in processes of care for caregivers and to support networking of members to practice and incorporate healing justice in their work. The goal of this was to serve as catalysts for the creation of practice-based guides that encourage cultures of care and develop reimaginations of the future of care.
Anna Young
Stealth medical device empowerment, co-founder MakerHealth, co-founder Pop Up Labs
José Gómez-Márquez
Innovator in Residence, Emory University Hatchery Center for Innovation, co-founder MakerHealth

Young and Gómez-Márquez co-led an RWJF-funded project (2020-2022) to support front-line nurse and clinician makers in creating solutions to supply shortages to treat patients and protect themselves during COVID-19. This project established two initiatives with the goal of supporting just-in-time solutions to save the lives of those without adequate equipment and supplies: 1) The Proto Program, which provided prototyping, mentorship, and educational resources to support 25 clinicianled projects, and 2) MakerNurse fellowships, which provided 25 individual nurses with prototyping tools, “Creation Kits,” and learning content to build their health technology ideas.
Uma Viswanathan, MA
President of the Samuel S. Fels Fund, previously the executive director of New Pluralists

Viswanathan led a grant supporting RWJF as an Affiliate Partner (2023-2024) with New Pluralists, a funder collaborative advancing a culture of pluralism. Through this partnership, RWJF learned with and grew connections to New Pluralists, which helped RWJF shape strategies and initiatives based on mutual interests, perspectives, and networks. This grant supported and helped RWJF attend funder and field-builder gatherings with a goal to influence philanthropy to invest in the organizations advancing pluralism, and to commit to upholding pluralism within their institutions and through their own grantmaking practices.

body.text: We Need More People Imagining More Futures
Without new visions we don’t know what to build, only what to knock down.
Robin D.G. Kelley, Freedom Dreams:- The Black Radical Imagination

children.text: Why We Need More People Imagining More Futures

body.text: We are living in complex times that regularly challenge our assumptions and disrupt political and social systems.

That’s why it is important for all of us to develop “futures literacy”—a set of skills that, like reading and writing, anyone can hone. UNESCO calls futures literacy an essential competency for the 21st century, observing that “being futures-literate empowers the imagination. It enhances our ability to prepare, recover and invent in the face of change.”

Futuring doesn’t need to be an all-in, all-the-time activity. It can be something people or organizations dip in and out of—perhaps with help from people such as the interviewees featured in this report. Not every person in every organization needs to be futuring full time, and there are ways to build speculative visioning practices and tools into everyday routines.

Spending time in the future may sound abstract—and it can be. Spending time in the future can also be fun. Creating opportunities for joy, healing, and immersion, futuring can unlock new solutions and reveal emerging obstacles in ways that traditional strategic planning or organizing often doesn’t.
We offer five of the many reasons we have learned that we should all be spending more time with more people anticipating and imagining more futures:
The Future Is Ours to Make

To influence the future, we must develop a healthy relationship with it. We must not turn away from possible futures that worry or frighten us but turn toward the futures we want. Fighting oppression and inequity in the present is vital; spending time in the future we’re fighting for fuels the work. The alternative is to continue to inhabit futures that others assert for us that serve their own interests.
Why We Need More People Imagining More Futures

children.text: Being here in Silicon Valley in particular, it is obvious that there are some people who basically think they are creating everybody else’s future and feel very empowered to create that future. Everybody else ends up living in these people’s futures… and it happens to them rather than happens in terms of something that they build.” —Marina Gorbis

body.text: The Future Shines a Light on Our Present

When we spend time imagining and anticipating futures where everyone thrives, we equip ourselves to analyze the present in critical and inspiring ways. Looking back from even a short visit to a liberatory future can help us see our emerging futures more clearly and put present inequities in stark relief. This can reveal the ways in which dominant narratives about what is possible often control or limit imagination.

children.text: For so many of us, we can’t imagine what it means to not have those struggles. We know the fight; we don’t know what victory is.” —Lafeyette Cruise

body.text: The Future Can Help Connect Us

Unleashing imagination and working together to dream healthy futures can be a path toward talking across differences. Speculative visioning creates distance—a safer place for questioning assumptions and hearing others. When we are together in an imagined future, the stakes are not as high— and we may be more willing to play. And it’s energizing to be in a place of possibility: Leaving the present behind for a moment can ease pressure and friction.

children.text: I think it’s actually fear of the future that’s driving a lot of the toxicity right now. If people actually felt they had a healthier relationship with the future and felt like they could at least talk to each other about it in a different way… if we could create space for people to dream together… it could bring the temperature down in a significant way… and allow us to imagine something quite different.” —Uma Viswanathan

body.text: Why We Need More People Imagining More Futures

children.text: Why We Need More People Imagining More Futures

body.text: The Future Needs Our Love

If we want to get to a sustainable future, we have to be in a meaningful relationship with it. Anticipating and collectively envisioning healthy futures takes time, care, and cooperation. We, individually and collectively, have to fall in love with the futures we want to emerge. When we do, we’ll want to take care of those futures, work hard to nurture our path toward them—removing barriers along the way so they become healthier places to inhabit, giving them what they need to thrive. We’ll want to take loving action today to keep that future alive and on track. Yes, it’s a long-distance relationship, and it has its ups and downs, but life just wouldn’t be the same without it.

children.text: There are many futures that you could imagine. I don’t think you want to support people in imagining the future or helping others imagine the future in a way that promotes hate, divisiveness, inequity, or ill health.” —Julia Mossbridge

body.text: The Future Can Offer Hope and Healing

Engaging with the future in a critical and imaginative way can be refreshing and regenerative. It can help turn conversations from registers of despair to registers of hope. Placing ourselves in desired futures can also provide health benefits. It can connect us with our childhood selves—the time when we could imagine freely without judgment. Spending time in a liberatory future can be a part of a healing journey for traumas caused by structural bias.

children.text: What is the culture in which people actually feel safe and they feel like their kids are safe? That’s a question I’m asking people all the time. It’s like: ‘Think of whoever’s your worst enemy. Now imagine that your grandchildren can play together. How do we get there so that they don’t feel scared of each other?’” —adrienne maree brown

body.text: Why We Need More People Imagining More Futures
We hope that our work will bring into being a future where we are living according to values of love, regeneration, and interdependence.

These values live in all of us but often our structures invite us to act more on values of domination, extraction, and violence. In this future we have repaired our relationships with ourselves, our communities, our land, and our ancestors. We live in abundant communities of care and mutuality. We have learned to slow down. To reconnect with the land around us. To deeply know and love our plant and animal kin, our land formations. We have become stewards, not owners. We have reclaimed so many of our useful ancestral practices, not forgetting them even as we develop technology that supports us to live more healthy and easeful lives. We hold all life as sacred. We honor the gifts and offerings of every individual. We work together to ensure that everyone is healthy, housed, and fed. We embrace diversity and difference as opportunities to share and learn how we might live more beautiful lives. We have access to the skills of repair, of healing, at individual, community, and institutional levels. Repair is our way of life and our utmost goal. We are mindful about our impact on future generations. We take great pride in making sure that their lives are even better than ours. We value education as integral to a peaceful and healthy society and make sure that no essential services are limited by the volatility of financial markets. We have finally transcended race as a barrier to love and interdependence. While we cultivate and enjoy many different cultures as different ways of being on the planet, we no longer use race or ethnicity as a determinant of opportunity. We atone often for the years during which we created systems to harm others so that we do not repeat this in the future.

children.text: Democratizing. Imaginati. . Future.

body.text: The exercise of imagination is dangerous to those who profit from the way things are because it has the power to show that the way things are is not permanent, not universal, not necessary.
Ursula K. Le Guin, The Wave in the Mind

children.text: Democratizing. Imagination. Future.

body.text: What does it mean to democratize the imagination of the future?
We asked our interviewees to interrogate the phrase and talk about whether and how it resonated.

children.text: Many interviewees immediately felt a deep alignment with the phrase. They went on to identify ways it was embodied in their work.

body.text: “I think it implies that right now there’s a relatively undemocratic distribution of who gets invited to imagine the future… Currently, those positions are largely dominated by men, by white people, by people from the global minority or the global north and all that. And so if we want to build a better, more inclusive, ecologically sustainable, and just future or futures, then one of the things we have to do is shift who gets to spend lots of time on that imagination and get resourced and supportive and paid to do it,” shared Costanza-Chock.

“Being in a co-creative space and democratizing imagination, you really benefit everyone,” explained Sinclair, “because all of those perspectives come together to optimize what we can potentially do in our future, and mitigate the things that could be toxic and limit the potential of our future.”
“I learned very, very early that great ideas and perspectives come from anywhere,” said Ogbunu.

“There are certain fields that are maybe disproportionately involved in shaping the future or shaping our perception of what the future could be,” said Shillingford, such as innovation, technology, and culture. “Those fields for various reasons tend not to be very diverse. So, the future that’s being imagined isn’t reflective of the desires of all communities or of diverse communities, perspectives, etc. So I understand ‘democratizing the imagination of the future’ to mean that there’s equity in who gets to imagine the future, whose perspectives, whose pasts shape what the future is, whose experiences inform what the future needs to be and whose desires for the
Democratizing. Imagination. Future.
future end up being what we all experience.”

“When I think about democratizing the imagination of the future, I think about what it actively requires of the organizers … a kind of constant self-interrogation and engagement with community in ways that disrupt power relations,” said Nissen. “If that doesn’t happen, then I think there’s always the risk of a kind of superficiality.”

“It’s about: How do we really think through inserting people that are marginalized now and centering them in the future so that we can think about the structures that need to be in place to facilitate that, and really sit with the reality and possibility of that world,” said Cruise.
“I learned very, very early that great ideas and perspectives come from anywhere,” said Ogbunu.

“Right now, the professional futurism community is heavily white, futures work in philanthropy, heavily, heavily, heavily, heavily, heavily white. Or non-Black. And I want to see the work we are doing. We need the resources, we need the platforms, we need the amplification that others are privileged to have that we have to work so hard to do,” said Bickham. “And not only do I think there could be a cross-fertilization among all of us, I think how we look at it is different and will provide a framework, a lens, a sensibility that will help shape the futurism sector locally to globally. I fundamentally believe that what’s coming out of these communities that have not been at the table is what our sector needs. It has a different set of principles, typically. It has a different set of methodologies, both codified and uncodified.”
Some broke the phrase down into its component parts, keying in on a particular word, or dissecting each word in turn.
Democratizing

A few interviewees noted the politicization of the word “democratizing.” They warned that it might be too closely associated with what many see as a broken system of democracy in America—especially in terms of the country’s history of structural racism and colonization. Mossbridge challenged the term “democratizing” as too restrictive. “If you think about what E pluribus unum really means, it is ‘out of many, one,’” she said. “So is that what you really want, or do you want from many, many?”
Democratizing. Imagination. Future.

children.text: Democratizing. Imagination. Future.

body.text: Bickham took a different tack, tying the term to the long history of participatory and activist movements in society and politics. “Those movements require tremendous vision, tremendous courage, sometimes tremendous mistakes. But they’re all about not only the expansion of the imagination— but the humanizing of democracies, and intellectual freedoms,” Bickham said. He described environmental, civil rights, gender equality, and similar organizing efforts as “an advancement and an antithesis of restrictive imaginations that have disempowered, dehumanized, and codified people’s possibilities.”

Brooks went even further back in time: “To democratize the future is to center African and Indigenous perspectives. And that leverages what I call ‘ancestral intelligence,’ the real AI. This ancestral intelligence has gone through forms of erasure that we need to recover and reclaim, leverage forward, for the eight billion people on the planet.”
Most interviewees interpreted the word “democratizing” as highlighting the urgent need to expand who is resourced to imagine the future, and the lost opportunity when this does not happen.

“People of color I think are best positioned for the future. So, the future needs us to be flexible and culturally competent and have the ability to sort of remix multiple technologies together to create new things. People of color are amazing at that, and they’re not well served by the current systems,” said Harris. “And so I think that these communities can really show us a different future if they have access to futurism tools and if they have space to radically imagine a different future.”
“To democratize the future is to center African and Indigenous perspectives. And that leverages what I call ancestral intelligence, the real AI.”

“I do think in terms of creating the imagination of the future,” said Shillingford, “Black communities are definitely left out, or Black working class communities don’t have as much access to those tools or other types of spaces where that happens.”

“How do you give [people] tools so that it doesn’t just stay in fairly privileged sectors?” Gómez-Márquez asked. Anna Young, who co-founded MakerHealth with him, expanded on this idea: “How do you truly make something not just available but
Democratizing. Imagination. Future.
accessible? A lot of that is understanding the constraints or barriers that might be facing the people who you’re trying to reach.”

At the time of the interview, Viswanathan was dealing with the question of democratizing directly in her work with the New Pluralists. When asked who might be excluded from, or actively opt out of, futuring practices, beyond groups that have been historically disadvantaged by bias such as people of color, women, and LGBTQ+ people, she said that it is important to consider particular “types of psychographs that seem missing... across the spectrum of conservatism, traditionalism, faith, etc.”

“When I think about what this phrase means to me, I think about its relationship with power,” she said, “because there’s so much about power that’s about having the ability to shape reality the way that you would like to be able to shape it.”
How do you truly make something not just available but accessible?
Imagination

Several interviewees reflected on the important relationship between imagination, narrative change, and social change. They noted that the stories we consume and tell as a culture shape our horizons of possibility. Narratives inscribe assumptions and prejudices in ways that make them seem natural rather than socially constructed. Speculative and science fiction approaches to literature, film, and artistic productions allow for critique of existing power relationships, and the exploration of new paradigms and possibilities. Imagined futures can also increase representation in genres that have previously been dominated by authors and heroes who are white men, and challenge related stereotypes.

“We are living inside of someone else’s imagination for how the world would work and we need to exercise our own imaginations in order to come to a different way of structuring the world, a different way of understanding our role in it,” said brown. Her own work includes writing speculative fiction—she recently published the final book in the Grievers trilogy, set in a deserted alternate Detroit in the aftermath of a pandemic called “Syndrome H-8.”

“I grew up in a storytelling system in the 1970s, you know the American version,” said Brooks. “Great tropes about science fiction but really centering white people. Lieutenant Uhura was one face that brightened up that spectrum and certainly Sun
Democratizing. Imagination. Future.

children.text: Democratizing. Imagination. Future.

body.text: Ra inspired films. But I didn’t see myself in these stories about the future… they just didn’t fully represent the spectrum of my experience. And I wanted to create an intentional ecosystem that would be more divergent, more interesting, more engaging, represent the cultures that have had visions of the future for millennia, and really put those into perspective more.”

“I came of age watching Star Trek and Star Wars and seeing the reaction to the racialized characters and actors in the sequels that came out a few years ago,” said Cruise. “And this question of ‘We’ve created a framework for you to imagine an intergalactic space world, aliens, death stars, but you can’t imagine a Black person with lines? You can’t imagine an Asian woman with lines? That’s where the limits of your imagination are?’ So, for me it is about this practice of stories we tell influence who we’re able to imagine, they set the tropes that we are allowed to build off of. But those tropes, those stories are predictive. And so that prediction is applied to the mind, whether you’re thinking about the city you live in or the fantasy world that you’re engaging with.”
“We’ve created a framework for you to imagine an intergalactic space world, aliens, death stars, but you can’t imagine a Black person with lines? You can’t imagine an Asian woman with lines?”

Some questioned the fealty to “imagination” rather than creation, and thought they should be merged as a joint endeavor of “imagination and creation” of the future. Gómez- Márquez suggested that it’s “imaginators”—the humans doing the imagining—who should be democratized, not imagination itself. “Because imagination by itself doesn’t exist. It’s not like the atmosphere, right? It’s literally something we create when we work at it,” he said.
“The invisible world produces the visible world. So, the richness of one’s imagination has a direct capacity to effectuate change and build new worlds.”
most of my life in the imagination world. And by that, I mean
In contrast, Bickham asserted that imagination is tantamount to creation. “To me, imagination is a form of prayer. I’ve spent
Democratizing. Imagination. Future.

believing and seeing, and sometimes weeping and not seeing, but knowing that the physical manifestation of the world is driven by the interior and imaginative capacity. That could be spirituality, it could be all kinds of things… The invisible world produces the visible world. So, the richness of one’s imagination has a direct capacity to effectuate change and build new worlds.”
Some respondents reflected on the question: What do we lose when entire segments of people are left out of the larger discourse about and processes to imagine futures?

“Historically the visions of the future of the most marginalized communities, because they implied their own freedom, brought about the biggest transformations in society for the better of all,” noted Shillingford. “And so these communities being left out of these formal fields of imagining the future, was actually a loss for all of humanity. And I feel like we see some of the consequences of that now. So whether it’s, we’re talking about poor communities, communities of color, queer communities, trans communities, etc., when those folks aren’t formally involved in imagining the future that gets structured and built, then we’re not creating as equitable futures and healthy futures as we could.”
Historically, the visions of the future of the most marginalized communities, because they implied their own freedom, brought about the biggest transformations in society for the better of all.

“I think that when it comes to imagining the future, we do have sometimes an unspoken belief that there are only some people who are supposed to be visionaries,” Lord said. “There are only some people who are supposed to be the ones who think about what policy is going to be for the future and what structures are going to be like for the future. And there are others who think that they don’t have that power, they don’t have that influence, and if they did try to imagine something, it would just be for fun because who’s going to take that and run with it?”
Democratizing. Imagination. Future.
ren
What I most strongly want to see in our future is a foundational belief in the intrinsic worth of everything in this biosphere: human and non-human, living and inanimate, crafted and natural.

I look forward to when our social and economic systems stop gauging an individual’s value by their ability to labor, produce, or be decorative, and no longer restrict their access to rights and resources accordingly. I want us to find harmony with our external environment. A colleague made a passing reference to sacred infrastructure, the idea that we could feel as moved by welldesigned, well-placed windmills and bridges as we are by mountains and seas. I thought it was lovely—not merely conservation of the ren natural environment but harmonizing our built environment to its location and purpose. Ultimately, I want us to work and live towards a post-scarcity world, where there is no longer any need for governments or individuals to commit excesses out of desperation that those things essential to our survival and wellbeing will no longer be available. Even scarcity-anxiety will no longer be exploitable by people who invent false value and false scarcity to channel wealth from real needs to manufactured desires (tulip mania!).

And, finally, even as we work towards optimizing our lives, I hope we can learn to make peace with the end of life. An unhealthy obsession with longevity/immortality, legacy, and procreation is rooted in a fear of death and dissolution, and when governments or rich men act on that fear, it can lead to unwanted consequences.

children.text: Democratizing. Imagination. Future.

body.text: Future
Futuremaking. Futurist. Strategic foresight. Afrofuturism. Scenario planning. World-building. Indigenous Futurism. Futures literacy.

The field of futuring is rife with jargon that can be inaccessible to those outside the practitioner community. And there is mixed agreement within the field on preferred terms. While there is an Association of Professional Futurists, even many within the profession still debate how they should describe themselves and their work.

One of the questions that popped up numerous times over the course of this research is whether the terms “futurism” or “futurist” are accurate or appropriate. Some expressed concern that the language of “the future” may inadvertently reinforce a singular, predetermined vision, rather than an imagining of a multiplicity of possible futures.

Through this work, we learned that the term “futurist” can be alienating for various reasons. Critics note its association with a 20th century Italian art movement that supported fascism. And, when people do associate it with professional futurists, they run up against stereotypes: People might think “that only old white guys can be futurists,” as Harris suggested. Additionally, some newer practitioners may feel that they don’t have the power, expertise, or influence to claim the term.

One of the ways in which we democratize the future is to get more people into a mindset of thinking broadly about where they want to be and where they want their communities in the world to be in the future.

Burse suggested that many people are already practicing foresight without even knowing it. “I see myself in 15 years being a pioneer on how we talk about strategic foresight in the African-American community,” he said. “Right now I think there’s a significant barrier in my community when it talks about futurism. I think we do it. We just don’t know we’re doing it. And the people that aren’t doing it, they’re not doing it because they don’t know what it is.”
Lord echoed the idea that people might already be doing this work without identifying themselves with the field. “One of the
Democratizing. Imagination. Future.

ways in which we democratize the future is to get more people into a mindset of thinking broadly about where they want to be and where they want their communities in the world to be in the future. This is something that comes naturally to some people and does not come naturally to others,” she said.

As is the case in any field, practitioners—including those interviewed for this report—regularly coin new terms to differentiate themselves from others who have come before or to call out a particular aspect of their work. You’ll read in the chapters that follow about “Future Architects” who are not drafting building plans; “Prophetic Innovation” that combines social justice, creative imagination, and collaborative action; and “AfroRithms from the Future” that center Black and Indigenous perspectives in future storytelling.
“Let’s have a million terms for different futurist things and let a million futures bloom, right? Let’s create a simpler language, but also a very divergent one too.”

Some of our interviewees like to use the word “futurist,” and some hate it. Some prefer “foresight,” while others feel that term is too narrowly associated with practices honed by professional consultants whose work aims to inform organizational or government strategy, or is focused on maximizing future profit.

We have no doubt the conversations about how to describe this work will continue—and that this might be the very best thing, as interviewee Brooks suggested: “Various cultures will help transform the language of futures thinking from a very kind of erudite academic exploration to more of an accessible, democratic, imagination language that people can speak and that we can develop. Let’s have a million terms for different futurist things and let a million futures bloom, right? Let’s create a simpler language, but also a very divergent one too.”

Cruise tied these thoughts together, saying: “I think there is an obligation to create the space for people to imagine. I think there’s an obligation for people to be given the skill set and practice to be imaginative, but I also think there’s an obligation for the people who have the democratic sense of power as citizens to participate in that democracy. So as a person who resides in this place, there’s a rhetorical imperative for you to imagine different futures.”
Democratizing. Imagination. Future. 47
I hope to see a future where the words/terms futurist and strategic foresight are no longer foreign to not only African-American communities, but all communities.

One of my lofty goals is to develop a strategic foresight digital curriculum that can be introduced to third-graders and tracked with a horizon of at least 12 years. Why is this important? Thinking about the future means learning to think differently. Thinking differently and embracing emerging change challenge our current assumptions, and over time today’s decisions, policies, and products will become obsolete. What does it look like when we take this ‘futureproof’ approach and introduce it at a young age? What happens when we challenge the way we think early, and expand the ability and opportunity to apply a futureproof mindset?
Cres ton
Bran don
I aim for a world with a healthy disregard for disciplinary boundaries.

Without gatekeeping, one where thinkers are encouraged to engage nontraditional ideas. So many current structures underline this sort of multiplicity, and I think it comes at the expense of better solutions to the world’s problems, and answers to longstanding mysteries.
Disruption
Creates Possibility
Building imaginative capacity is an essential collective project if we hope to successfully navigate the tidal waves of change that have already been unleashed.
Ed Finn, Issues in Science and Technology

children.text: When Disruption Creates Possibility

body.text: Recent years have been filled with disruption.

A global pandemic that exposed deep social and economic inequities, a widespread racial reckoning that called institutions across the country to account for their history of systemic bias and exclusion, crises related to climate change, and the rising threat of authoritarianism in the U.S. and abroad. All of this has contributed to an environment of what many are now terming a “polycrisis,” in which “disparate crises interact such that the overall impact far exceeds the sum of each part.”

This volatile environment has produced an increase in demand for futures thinking in philanthropy and social innovation. This made Robert Wood Johnson Foundation’s role as a funder that has prioritized innovation and foresight for many years more visible and relevant. As the Chronicle of Philanthropy reported in February 2024, “Futurism is having a moment in postpandemic foundation strategy.” This means that the Futures team’s explorations and investments in practitioners and projects that are democratizing the imagination of the future are well positioned to inform other funders and social innovators.

children.text: After 2020, “across the world and across the social sector, people realized that the future was not what they thought it was going to be,” said Harris, “and that all of these conversations that we had been having about rapid pace of change and big transformations on the horizon, and how we need to be ready for them, people were like, ‘Oh yeah.’ And the idea that the future doesn’t happen to you, we create it with the decisions that you make today—it was actually a much more intensive responsibility for the field.

body.text: The pandemic period and widespread interrogation of structural racism also cracked open a window for deeper inquiries into health and disability justice, radical visioning, somatic approaches to wellness, compassion, mutual aid, and the importance of self-care and rest. For many, this period served as what novelist and social critic Arundhati Roy called a “portal” to new possibilities and profound shifts
When Disruption Creates Possibility in social relations.

“Historically, pandemics have forced humans to break with the past and imagine the world anew,” she wrote in 2020 for the Financial Times. “This one is no different. It is a portal, a gateway between one world and the next. We can choose to walk through it, dragging the carcasses of our prejudice and hatred, our avarice, our databanks and dead ideas, our dead rivers and smoky skies behind us. Or we can walk through lightly, with little luggage, ready to imagine another world. And ready to fight for it.”

In response to rising global and social instability, more and more governments and institutions have been turning to foresight. For more than a decade, UNESCO has been supporting the creation of futures literacy labs around the world. The United Nations identified strategic foresight as a “core capability” in its 2.0 plan released in 2023, and other multilateral organizations such as the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) and the EU have been working to embed it into their policymaking and governance mandates. Government agencies of numerous countries have also incorporated future-oriented capacities into their work, as the Copenhagen Institute for Futures Studies details in their proposal titled An Alliance for the Future.
“Historically, pandemics have forced humans to break with the past and imagine the world anew.”
How is philanthropy adopting futuring?

These shifts have been happening as foundations are seeking solutions to increasingly complex societal challenges. Foundations such as RWJF, Democracy Fund, and the Omidyar Network have similarly been exploring and applying futures thinking and speculative visioning in an effort to bring about more desirable futures for the causes they focus on and the people they serve.

A number of reports and new collaborative efforts by foundations and philanthropic organizations have documented this work, illuminating the ways that this sector has pivoted to learn and incorporate such skills. Key examples include:

items.text: Rockefeller Philanthropy Advisors’ Foresight & Futures Initiative
Joseph Rowntree Foundation’s Imagination Infrastructures

The Democracy Futures Project led by Philanthropy for Active Civic Engagement

body.text: Some foundations and futuring practitioners begin their journey with formal foresight training from institutions and leading thinkers such as IFTF, the Future Today Institute (Now known as the Future Today Strategy Group), the University of Houston’s foresight master’s program or the School of International Futures. But many are exploring the wider futuring ecosystem and methods.
Who gets to talk about the future? Who gets to predict its path? And who gets to advocate for a particular unfolding of the future?
Calls to democratize the futuring field

In the past five years, there has been a groundswell of thinkers and writers advocating for the importance of shifting foresight and imagination into more inclusive, participatory, and decolonizing practices.

For example, “Who gets to talk about the future? Who gets to predict its path? And who gets to advocate for a particular unfolding of the future?” asks Danielle Logue in a 2023 article for the Stanford Social Innovation Review. “In short, who does future-making as a practice, and how do they do it? These questions are important to answer given how powerful imagined futures are in driving economic activity and social impact.”

In a 2025 study titled Advancing Futures Studies: A Mixed-Methods Approach to Identifying and Addressing Critical Research Gaps, researcher and consultant Alireza Hejazi identified that, “the overrepresentation of Global North perspectives creates a foresight monoculture that risks irrelevance in a multipolar world. Closing these gaps requires futures studies to evolve from a ‘discipline of the powerful’ to a ‘discipline of the pluriverse.’ Futures researchers need to
When Disruption Creates Possibility center marginalized voices as equal partners in shaping tomorrow.”

Countering the idea of forging a singular vision of the future— for example, see the stories of gloom that have accompanied the rise of artificial intelligence over the past few years— foundations are exploring ways to support and collaborate with grantees to imagine multiple transformed future societies which center health, justice, and liberation.

This approach honors the work of the many people and communities who have been not just visioning but practicing their own versions of preferable futures for many generations—for example, Indigenous Peoples across the Americas, or those advancing the concept of Sankofa from Ghana. Contemporary philosophies and movements such as Afrofuturism and Indigenous Futurism incorporate and celebrate longstanding futuring practices of Indigenous and Black people. They engage ancient traditional and ancestral knowledge, technologies, and culture, as well as what Dr. Ambelin Kwaymullina describes as “deep understandings of oppressive systems.”

“Afrofuturism challenges the foundations of what we call ‘the future.’ It rejects the a-historic narratives of clean-slate innovation and insists instead on ancestral reckoning,” writes Brooks with co-author Reynaldo Anderson in a 2025 article in The Guardian. “It knows that futures are made, not found—and that who gets to imagine them is a political question.”

Movements such as Queer Futurisms and Crip/Disabled Futurisms have also been vital spaces for centering the experiences of LGBTQIA+ and disabled people while countering structural injustices and expanding possibilities for tomorrow.
“Afrofuturism challenges the foundations of what we call ‘the future.’”

More and more, efforts to imagine and bring about transformed futures are exemplified by vital action taking place outside of formal institutions, such as the movements and efforts of organizers, activists, and nonprofits that were catalyzed by the impacts of COVID-19 and national uprisings in response to the murder of George Floyd. People working to support one another through mutual aid efforts, resisting oppression and hate, and moving resources in ways that work toward just and liberated futures are also doing
When Disruption Creates Possibility

children.text: When Disruption Creates Possibility

body.text: groundbreaking applied futures thinking.

Examples of social justice leaders engaging in imagining more just futures and pathways to them include the Social and Economic Justice Leaders Project, Careful Industries, the Horizons Project, Governance Futures, and more.
“… the inadequacy and harms of traditional foresight demand an expansion of methods, imaginations, and philosophies…”

In the futures studies field, critical futures practitioners such as Sohail Inayatullah have been challenging the idea of a singular future predicated on values of growth and status quo, asking questions such as those shared by Jennifer Gidley in a 2016 paper titled Understanding the Breadth of Futures Studies Through a Dialogue with Climate Change: “Whose future is being predicted? Whose science is being used to measure the trends? Who decides what is preferred?”

For experts calling for the decolonization of futures thinking and practice, the inadequacy and harms of traditional foresight demand an expansion of methods, imaginations, and philosophies, and interrogating what Aarathi Krishnan calls “patterns of power” in ways that move from participatory visioning to intentional “systems transformation efforts” in the book Sacred Civics: Building Seven Generation Cities. See Decolonizing Futures: Exploring Storytelling as a Tool for Inclusion in Foresight as another example. This also dovetails with parallel work on decolonizing philanthropy and shifting focus on funding and work to achieve liberation, such as the Right Relations Collaborative.
Imagination on the rise

Imagination has also risen in the fields of philanthropy and social change as a tool to shape the worlds we want to live in. The concept has surfaced in the context of efforts to grow the field and practices of imagination broadly (Imagination Infrastructures), catalyze new visions for philanthropy (Freedom Dreams in Philanthropy), and even promote change across the UN system (The Most Creative Look to the Future).

Riel Miller is a leading global expert on the practice of using futures to both build strategies and to anticipate possibilities well beyond the scope of our current conceptions of what’s possible. He served as the head of foresight and futures literacy at UNESCO in Paris from 2012–2022. In the

When Disruption Creates Possibility foreword to the 2024 book Designing Futures: Speculation, Critique, Innovation, he writes about the importance of not just extrapolating futures from current trends, which can perpetuate harmful dynamics, but of “liberating perception from the blinkers of expectation.”

In her 2024 book Imagination: A Manifesto, Ruha Benjamin writes: “Imagination is a field of struggle, not an ephemeral afterthought that we have the luxury to dismiss or romanticize. [This book] is a proposal for exorcising our mental and social structures from the tyranny of dominant imaginaries. It is a field guide for seeding an imagination grounded in solidarity, in which our underlying interdependence as a species and with the rest of the planet is reflected back at us in our institutions and social relationships.”

Benjamin notes that governments, corporations, and universities are central to maintaining narratives that “continue to infect our collective imagination, distorting how we see and value different groups, cultures, and worldviews. They presuppose a fixed genetic or cultural predisposition that ignores the plasticity of our brains and the malleability of our social relations.”

Walidah Imarisha, an assistant professor at Portland State University who collaborated with brown to edit the 2015 anthology Octavia’s Brood: Science Fiction Stories from Social Justice Movements observed that “Whenever we try to envision a world without war, without violence, without prisons, without capitalism, we are engaging in speculative fiction.”

The Protopia Futures framework invites a critical examination of futurist practice and discourse and offers principles that aim to center perspectives from those who have systematically been disadvantaged by structural racism or identity-based prejudices. In doing so, Protopia Futures framework founder monika bielskyte notes the hope is “to create spaces of active imagining, resourcing in the present and moving towards collaborative visions of liberation.”

This demonstrates why imagining alternative worlds and engaging in methods that expand the range of what is possible cannot and should not be contained within a siloed foresight profession or a particular culture. The School of International Futures (SOIF)’s Next Generation Foresight Practitioners is leading a project titled Futures Methods From Around the World, which they describe as an effort to decenter “non-Indigenous Global North frames” and highlight the
When Disruption Creates Possibility

body.text: range of global futures methods and traditions to think “more broadly and radically about our shared futures as a society.”

Community members and families who aspire to be mobile and flourishing will be able to impact and navigate spiritual, social, technological, economic, environmental, political, and legal signals, trends, and countertrends.

These families will have futurism and Prophetic Innovation as core or as common as banking or recreation or work. Babies will know no other ethos except one in which any system or institution, beginning with their home life, proliferates with self-determination, foresight, and holistic flourishing in which generational wealth is both economic and moral/humanitarian legacies. Families will be their own futurists both in their domestic trajectory but ecosystems in which imagination, justice, democratic belonging, and compassion will be propulsive and dignified, rather than elusive and humiliating. These enlightened communities and individuals will embody and prophesy to the world that there will be no more war and devastation in a world of consummate peace in which the lion will lie down with the lamb, every exalted mountain will be made low, and every crooked place made smooth.
Practicing Participation: Let’s Go the Future

Futuring processes that aim to create more just futures have the potential to be better informed, and create a stronger sense of possibility, if they can successfully embrace a greater diversity of knowledges and perspectives.
The Australian Center for Social Innovation (TACSI)’s National Futures Initiative

children.text: Practicing Participation: Let’s Go to the Future

body.text: Participation in democratizing the imagination of the future takes many different forms—the examples below from interviewees offer models for others about how to do it in their own work.

“How do we create the conditions under which people can engage with these things in less elite settings?” asked Gómez- Márquez. “How do you train more of this style of convening and thinking in places that are already embedded in the community?” Learning through their work in healthcare settings, MakerHealth co-founder Young adds that we must navigate existing power structures, noting that it is important to meet people where they are, and to “understand the social dynamics of the clinicians’ work with their peers and how that impacts their ability to come and participate in creative thinking, in more imaginative work.”

children.text: I think futuring is one of the most important leadership actions that we can take. But it matters who’s brought in. If we only engage the same groups of people, we’ll just reiterate what we’ve already built, and pass around stale ideas and structures that we’ve been building from,” noted Viswanathan.

body.text: “What does it mean for futures to be designed with, for, and by people who do not usually have the luxury of time and space to even think about what their dreams are for the future?” she asked. “That’s where we will get the fresh and relevant new thinking we need.”

Moving the conversations about futures outside of elite institutions and making them accessible and available to people in many different settings has been central to the work of several of the interviewees. Crucially, this work is about engaging people in meaningful and participatory ways that build trust and shift unequal power dynamics. “I think you’ll never have full democratization of ideas or information insofar as elitism and prestige is so dominant,” said Ogbunu.
Practicing Participation: Let’s Go to the Future

Participation is intrinsic to this work, not only to generate a wider range of possible futures but to build capacity, insight, and epiphanies within particular communities or fields that can be shared beyond the confines of the experience.

Burse and Bickham, at Jackson Medical Mall, engaged youth and community members in Jackson, Mississippi, in activities to imagine new futures of work through play. Launched in January 2020, the Young Futurists Project engages young people through an “innovation hub impacting the culture of health through technology, social solutions, and creativity.” The program provides a range of life skills and workforce development training to people from 8–18 years old, aiming to support “youth technologists, creative innovators, and advocates with skills to address current and future emerging issues.” The Young Futurists Project engages more than 40 young people each year in afterschool and summer camp programs through courses that not only address futurefocused topics such as strategic foresight, virtual reality, and artificial intelligence, but also culinary, visual, and performing arts, creative content, foreign language, quilting and sewing, dance, and entrepreneurial training.
“I think this is one of the most important leadership actions that we can actually be doing.”

Harris worked with a cohort of rural and Indigenous leaders in 2021 and 2022 to identify signals of the future that might inspire future visions in communities often left out of conversations about what comes next and embed futurism into their practice. This cohort, titled “Field Trips to the Future,” aimed to “strengthen the influence of future-oriented thinking with those working to ensure that communities and Native nations across the rural United States are healthy places where everyone belongs, lives with dignity, and thrives.” Leaders engaged in three distinct components of programming: 1) Discover, which involved synchronous three-hour virtual retreats over 12 months to build their knowledge and skills as future focused leaders; 2) Nurture, which assigned participants to small groups to encourage connection, ideation, and problem solving, and 3) Act, which supported them in producing short talks about relevant trends for their work, and how the community development field might harness them.
Nissen worked to build foresight capacity inside the field of social work. Organizing around the question “What
Practicing Participation: Let’s Go to the Future

children.text: Practicing Participation: Let’s Go to the Future

body.text: would it look like if social workers were futurists?” she led a fellowship and lab over 18 months as a “collaborative learning experiment.” Participants explored “anti-racist, equitable, and intersectionally relevant emerging and innovative possibilities and practices for social work and health.” From 2020 to 2022, social work scholars and leaders from a wide range of focus areas (e.g., food security, climate change, racial bias in emerging technologies, etc.) experimented with futures and foresight frameworks. Together, they investigated how futuring approaches might strengthen the impact of social workers as researchers, teachers, and in the field. Nissen and this cohort produced Shaping Social Work Futures: Utopias, Dystopias, and Everything in Between, a virtual forum in 2024, to foster national dialogue and sharing of ideas around futures frameworks and disseminate learning from the Lab. The forum provided delegates the opportunity to learn about futures thinking and foresight practice and surface meaningful aspirations and action steps to become more “future ready” as a profession.
What would it look like if social workers were futurists?

Mossbridge and her team at The Institute for Love and Time (TILT) invited people who are incarcerated to connect with their own future selves through a novel methodology. The Expanded Human Potential Pilot Program was a collaboration with Cook County Department of Corrections in 2022, designed to “integrate balanced time perspective, unconditional love, and positive wellness messages” into the daily and weekly habits of 14 men. Using TILT’s Time Machine app, the project invited participants to join a workshop with members of the TILT team. The Time Machine app provides three tools: 1) “a daily practice for recording and hearing messages generated by and for the user, allowing them to create a healthy relationship with themselves over time”; 2) a “meditation tent” that “allows people to record their own supportive meditations to play repeatedly throughout the day or just once in a while”; and 3) a “hope garden that provides a space for people to plant seeds of hope for their future, nourished by the resources they select.”

Brooks and the team at the Museum of Children’s Art (MOCHA) leverage futures thinking in their work with young people through the Community Futures School. This program combines Afrofuturism, Indigenous futurism, queer futurism, arts integration, and futures literacy to guide highschoolers to be able “to imagine futures where they matter and where they

Practicing Participation: Let’s Go to the Future count—futures where Black and Brown people are thriving— and then translate their visions into change, whether that’s cultural programming for their neighborhoods, legislative agendas for their communities, or another form of activism.” Brooks and his team work with students to word-build an inclusive Oakland in 2045. They begin by identifying present day “signals of the future” and thinking about how those might scale, and building stories about futures that center Black and Indigenous perspectives. They then develop artifacts from these futures for display in the museum. “When the kids see these artifacts, it makes it real for them. They know their vision of the future is possible,” said Nina Woodruff-Walker, MOCHA’s executive director.

These are only a few of the many rich and inventive ways that these pathbreaking interviewees have been democratizing the imagination of the future. As Bickham explained, “I’ve got to get people like creatives, technologists, and others, folks from the community to make it more vibrant and more alive and more futuristic, more innovative. And by participatory, I don’t just mean being at the table, I mean, how does this work come out in ways that are breakthrough and exciting and dynamic? I’m so tired of conferences and panels and discussions.”
Practicing Participation: Let’s Go to the Future
Lafa yette
