The future that I’m working to bring into existence is one of rest and spaciousness. Not a suburban spaciousness where things feel empty and sterile and disconnected. One where everything we design, create, build is embedded with a spaciousness to hold people for all that they are.

In the future that I imagine is one in which our time is spacious—rejecting industrial time in favor of ideas such as queer time or CPT. As an urban planner, I’m working towards a built environment that shows that sort of spacious love to all who encounter it, one that isn’t about controlling the body, increasing productivity, or efficiency; rather, built environments that see the beauty in the work of connecting, the work of being human, in relationship with the human, and more than human. A future built on spacious love doesn’t center the home-to-work transportation infrastructure; it has DIY spaces for art, culture, and production, it has streets where children feel love not just from the immediate family [but] from the entire community. There is space for curiosity and play at all ages; there is rest and revelry at every opportunity—confident that collectively we can do the work to ensure we all live well.

This future requires a cathedral approach— collective creativity and craftsmanship over multiple generations for the benefit of the future many. This world isn’t guided by a desire to be seen as a good person or the right side of history, rather it’s guided by people holding many truths and histories and directing our culture to actually ‘do good’.
Feeling Our Way in Futures
Imagined futures are, it seems to me, necessary ways of navigating the structural cruelties of the present– the post-colonial, post-slavery present.
Angela Davis, Planetary Utopias – Hope, Desire, Imaginaries in a Post-Colonial World

children.text: Feeling Our Way into Futures
What does it mean to feel our way into futures, why is it important, and how are our interviewees making it possible?

body.text: Often, strategic foresight is presented as an empirical exercise. However, feelings matter in this work. Efforts to rationally discuss possible trends and signals too often gloss over or ignore the inequities of the systems we live in and how those affect people.

In contrast, we can feel the futures we are exploring more intensely through stories and experiences that paint vivid pictures. Futuring processes that are co-creative and engage participants in exercising their own imaginations and drawing from the wells of their memories and perceptions are particularly powerful.

Novels, films, and games about the future that are embedded densely with characters, experiences, and dramatic twists can be compelling in ways that generate hope or foreshadow pivotal social changes. Some of the projects that interviewees we spoke with created build on common reference points from pop culture. Such approaches work only not because of the richness of description or depiction but the emotional resonance of the characters and the worlds being built— especially if they reflect our own identities and experiences.

Tangible or immersive experiences can help us to see what it’s like to inhabit and interact with a possible tomorrow. Some interviewees invite people to make artifacts that bring possible futures to life. We can travel to the shores of a possible future, pick up a metaphorical pebble, and bring it back with us to motivate our actions today. When we feel the futures we imagine, we remember them more viscerally, which can make us more likely to bring them into being.

Futuring can run the risk of being triggering—but also has the potential to be healing or cathartic. Many of these projects are unabashedly political. They illuminate the consequences of class, race, and power disparities. They reveal the long-term
Feeling Our Way into Futures implications of existing or potential ideologies and policies in a way that more drier and more analytical modes of analysis often don’t.
Co-creation and play
Methods that bring people in to co-create narratives, worlds, characters, and artifacts use storytelling or art to explore possible futures:

items.text: When Brooks invited youth in Oakland to play the AfroRithms from the Future game—a card deck that invites players to pick an inspiration, a tension, a system, and object drawn from Black and indigenous perspectives—he was asking them to co-create futures with archetypes and issues that relate to their own lives and identities.

When Nissen and members of the Social Work Health Futures Lab asked social workers to create Monuments of the Future, they were tapping into not just the rich and controversial history of monuments, but participants’ own desires around what future societies choose to memorialize when a social issue of their choice has been resolved.

When the Guild of Future Architects invited a diverse and multidisciplinary group of artists and social innovators to join a “Futurist Writers Room” and collaboratively imagine 100 years into the past and the future one decade at a time, they activated not only each participant’s imagination, but the collective wisdom of the group during a moment in the pandemic when many were seeking solace and solutions.

body.text: “How do we develop more organic, easeful models of play?”

Using co-creative methods in futuring is not just something nice-to-have or an afterthought—it is central to making the work of social change and community engagement more nuanced and relevant. However, for the process to work it is important to involve communities in a way that respects their time, comfort level, and expertise. “How do we develop more organic, easeful models of play?” asked Brooks.

An article in The Conversation titled “Collaboratively imagining the future can bring people closer together in the present,” notes that the benefits of co-imagining futures can include weaving new social relationships and establishing deeper connections, in addition to having “the potential to combat loneliness and disconnection.”
Feeling Our Way into Futures

children.text: Feeling Our Way into Futures

body.text: Tapping into pop culture

Often, in conversations about the future, participants in workshops will refer to popular science-fiction films or books in order to anchor the point they are making. Interviewees also cited such narratives as core to their own inspiration and understanding of what futures might be possible, or ones that society should work to prevent.

“One of the ways in which we democratize the future is to get more people into a mindset of thinking broadly about where they want to be and where they want their communities in the world to be in the future,” said Lord. “Certain kinds of literature help to encourage that kind of thinking.”

Ogbunu spoke about how conversations with his mother about Star Trek served as a gateway into discussing larger issues, inspiring his own desire to leverage pop culture for social critique and to serve as a bridge between academic research and public debate. He uses the 1997 movie Gattaca to motivate discussions about the futures that innovations in genetics may permit. Shillingford described how seeing the response to the Black Panther film in 2018 inspired her and her collaborators to create speculative narratives. “We were like, ‘Oh, this community is deeply craving widely accessible images of an alternative world,’” she said. “The fact that people went to see this movie like six times on average because they just wanted to sit in that a little bit longer… we were like, okay, maybe there are not enough currently widely available experiences of other possibilities.”

A concerted investment in science fiction that is not necessarily super-dystopic and that has an intention, about the futures we desire,” said Shillingford. “I think that’s essential from diverse communities and scaled at the level of mass culture.”

“I often say we can live in a Hunger Games future, which it often feels like we’re closer and closer to, or we could live in a Star Trek future, and we decide which one is the direction that we’re moving toward,” said Harris. “But I think for people in the social sector, they can see how close we are to the Hunger Games future, that they haven’t even been given the space to hope for something different. And if they were allowed to sort of radically imagine what that future looks like, everybody wants that.
Feeling Our Way into Futures
Very few people want the Hunger Games… and I think there are a lot of people that, given the right tools, could really craft us a map to something much more beautiful.”

“A concerted investment in science fiction that is not necessarily super-dystopic and that has an intention, about the futures we desire,” said Shillingford. “I think that’s essential from diverse communities and scaled at the level of mass culture.”
Tangible tomorrows

In her interview, Gorbis highlighted “social simulations,” as used by her colleague Jane McGonigal at the Institute for the Future. These large-scale experiences invite participants to assume personas and “play” their way through scenarios of future disasters to understand systems and model responses. Sometimes these can be eerily prescient.

“For example, in 2010, IFTF invited thousands of people all over the world to imagine living in a global respiratory pandemic (the scenario was based on expert interviews and analysis of data that made this a likely possibility),” wrote Gorbis in “Using Strategic Foresight to Create the Future We Want,” a 2024 piece for the Stanford Social Innovation Review. “Essentially, we saw a preview of what took place in the real COVID-19 pandemic. Many participants reported to Jane, the main creator of the simulation, that when the real pandemic hit, they felt ready because they had already worked through this scenario.”
“Is there a particular kind of stress or pain that foresight professionals are under that we haven’t named yet?”

Shillingford described how she and the artists and makers she has worked with arrived at creating “really large-scale group experiences that create a sense of awe or connection.” She shared, “The work that we pivoted towards doing was: How do we bring back the tradition of imagining the future? We felt it’s a tradition, particularly within Black communities, to imagine the future, that Black communities had always dreamt of a future, that it was for the sake of their own liberation that led to liberation for other people, whether directly or by proxy or by inspiration.” In addition to protesting and resisting what is happening in the present, she said, they wanted to “bridge between activist visioning and cultural worldbuilding.”
Feeling Our Way into Futures

children.text: Feeling Our Way into Futures

body.text: To shape the worlds in their experiences, Shillingford shared that they establish premises and assumptions of the world, as well as “values, myths, or stories like rituals and ceremonies and celebrations, relations—like who’s in the world and how are they relating and the aesthetics of the world. What does it look, feel, sound, taste or smell like?” Once that is established, they think about: “How do the values and culture inform the systems and structures at a more complex level,” and then draft narratives from that world that feel important to tell.

“So what part of the world do we want to show from a story perspective? Is it a day in the life of someone who lives in this world, for example? And then we’ll start to pull artifacts, which for us could be objects, could be places, could be practices or experiences from that world that we want to make real. And then that’s what we try to translate in some way into the experience that’s built.”

Unlike many of the grants featured in this report, the MakerHealth process starts from a place of hands-on tinkering and problem solving rather than more cerebral speculation. “We really try to engage local communities to be creative and really future think: ‘Okay, can you build it?’ How can we really concretely think about this?” said Gómez-Márquez. “For us it's very tangible. Not a lot of people do that often enough. [Instead] It's a lot of convening and asking questions, and those gatherings look like a lot of post-it notes and sometimes whiteboards, and because the outputs end up often in something that sits on a coffee table, they don't really move the needle, beyond elite knowledge dissemination."
“So what part of the world do we want to show from a story perspective?”
Tuning in to hope while processing trauma

Helping people, communities, and organizations hold alternate and broader visions of their work and futures was a key theme across the interviews. As interviewees made space for grief, hope, and joy in their work, shifts in how people related to possible futures took place. Harris noted, “It’s also changing how people talk about the future—there’s more excitement and creativity about what’s possible.”

Depending on the topic and the goals, imagining futures can be profoundly disturbing, or energizingly fun. Futuring practitioners need to be aware of both possibilities and take responsibility for the ways in which they engage participants.
Feeling Our Way into Futures

“I found a lot of joy in middle schoolers and high schoolers,” said Harris. “There’s a time in childhood where you’re asked to imagine about the future and that muscle hasn’t atrophied yet… They just have these really interesting ideas and often don’t have the space or support to be able to tell those stories, but they’re most invested in the future. They’re going to spend the most time there.”

Cruise observed that younger participants can be itching for new visions. “I see a lot of young people [who are] just like, ‘Yeah, this is not tenable.’ Mostly because the story of the future that they’ve been raised on is that there is no future,” he said, and “if there is no future, why am I invested in maintaining what is happening here?”

“We have these conversations constantly in our minds about, ‘You’re not good enough, you’re an imposter, you don’t belong, you’re marginalized. Who do you think you are?” reflected Brooks. “That’s why I say these storytelling systems are so important because there are things that we can live into and they can, as generations go through these systems, see themselves as a part of a bigger conversation inspired by the trauma of our ancestors, but not just residing there.” Brooks suggested that the work of narrative, of “creating alternative memories of the future,” can help to heal trauma.
So futurism is about perspective. It’s not about ignoring the present, it’s about perspective on the present that allows you to pull up into hope.

"Futuring is complicated. You're doing a lot of pretty intense exploring with people, which includes considering both positive futures and scary ones. Some people get overwhelmed… even traumatized at the thought of facing sometimes frightening possibilities," suggests Nissen, who noted "just the act of sitting people down to try to think together intensively about these disruptors can trigger people."

She calls this "anticipatory trauma," and also mentioned the concept of "secondary trauma" sometimes experienced by futurists, asking, “Is there a particular kind of risk or stress that futurists face guiding people into [potentially] dark futures that we should prepare well for? Purposeful futures work supports people to move through all this to arrive at
Feeling Our Way into Futures

children.text: Feeling Our Way into Futures

body.text: the other side of foresight practice feeling a sense of hopefulness and agency. Is this a kind of "emotional labor" futurists need to be prepared to facilitate, that needs to be named and built into training—both for their wellbeing—and the wellbeing of groups engaged in the work?"

“So much of our work is precedented on brokenness and trauma and disparity, generational often. Fatigue is built off of the struggle of social change, let alone futuristic/ prophetically innovating change,” said Bickham. “How do you facilitate something most of us have never seen, at all levels of the ecosystem? I’m interested in finding methods, tools, experiments such as speculative fiction that help people move beyond the trauma, and delve into the unabashed creativity that’s there.”

Mossbridge’s work suggests ways in which people who have experienced trauma become disconnected from their past and future selves, which can be addressed through “time travel therapy,” in which those experiencing PTSD can process their trauma by imagining themselves going back in time to help their past selves, or devising routes to get to a more desirable state.
“We can travel to the shores of a possible future, pick up a metaphorical pebble, and bring it back with us to motivate our actions today.”

“So that’s how I do futurism,” she said. “I move to a stance in the future and build the being for the now in my mind who says: ‘Okay, now here’s what you have to do if you want to get there.’… So futurism is about perspective. It’s not about ignoring the present, it’s about perspective on the present that allows you to pull up into hope.”

Embodied experiences can be cathartic. Bickham reported participants coming out of his experiences with “a joy of learning and a joy of living. He noted “a friend and I are working on this concept called the Temple of Joy, in which our futurism work is led by joy, integrates joy and ends with joy, which means that it has to be celebratory and it’s physical. We dance, we sing, we do whatever.” Also engaging participants in joyful experiences rooted in music and dance, Shillingford noted that her team has been brainstorming ways for people to tap back into the catalytic feelings of awe or connection that are usually felt during their one-off events to support more sustained transformation.
Feeling Our Way into Futures
Ju lia
It’s the year 2030. And instead of social media being the dominant way that people interact with each other, there are two dominant ways.

One is in person—in communities, there’s a rediscovery of community. And even technology starts to be much more community-based, kind of like Meetup where what you’re doing with the technology is to leverage the geographic space and the resources in that community, and then people get together. So, it’s like a means to an end of human connection in the moment in the community. Our kids, like the world’s children, will know that the most important thing is connection and love and that the other pieces can fall away. So humanity’s deal where we think we always have to have war or we always have to have disconnection or we always have to have some kind of human hierarchy starts to just seem like, Wait, why? What was that about? Was that about justifying inadequacy? Was that about justifying slavery? What was that about? A real confusion about what that was about because we realized the immanence, and the imminence of love.
Resisting Futures: What’s Stopping ?
BIPOC leaders are being asked to simultaneously dismantle the past, survive in the present, and create an alternative future.
Neha Mahajan and Felicia Griffin, Nonprofit Quarterly

children.text: Resisting Futures: What’s Stopping Us?

body.text: Why might it be difficult for people to engage in futuring? While many studies suggest that humans are intrinsically future-facing, those seeking to invite conversations about the future sometimes encounter resistance.
Interviewees found that barriers can include:

items.text: Alienating jargon
Assumptions that futures thinking is about “predicting” the future
Concerns that futures thinking is too “woo woo” or mystical
Worries that spending time talking about the future ignores pressing present-day priorities
Feeling unqualified to imagine futures
Feeling uncomfortable or unqualified to be creative
Suspicion that talking about the future masks avoidance of or a desire to look past the inequities of the present
Depression or fear about the future, or a sense that there is “no future”

children.text: When people see the worst of what’s happening right now, they shut down. How do we psychologically overcome the fear of the unknown and the pain of change?” asked Sinclair.

body.text: Systemic bias
Structural “isms”—including discrimination based on race, class, gender, and education levels—continue to block people from
Resisting Futures: What’s Stopping Us?

participating in formalized futuring. Sinclair noted that access to spaces where imagining the future happens is not always equitable, and such spaces are often dominated by people with resources, who are often also white and economically secure. Other interviewees observed that youth and the elderly are also often left out of such discussions.

Imagining the future engages a wide variety of practices and modalities: art and creative practices, activism, participatory futuring, formal scenario planning and foresight, and beyond. Some of these might be widely available, while others more often take place in privileged settings or specific institutions such as government or universities. The context, access to resources, and opportunity to engage in futures thinking varies for each individual or organization doing the imagining. The stakes differ too, depending on whether resources are available and how dangerous it might be to posit futures that threaten those in power.

“Right now, there's a relatively undemocratic distribution of who gets invited to imagine the future… because of broader and underlying structural inequality along the lines of race, class, and gender and global distribution of wealth,” said Costanza-Chock. This includes “people with the most resources, people who occupy positions of power within cultural industries and policy spaces and storytelling and narrative and actually planning and foresight—pretty much any space where you're spending a lot of time trying to imagine, design, and plan for what the future might look like.”
“Access to spaces where imagining the future happens is not always equitable, and such spaces are often dominated by people with resources, who are often also white and economically secure.”

Few organizations have dedicated access to space, time, social or political capital, or money to rigorously and regularly practice foresight. What’s more, the futures they imagine might not be valued in a larger discussion shaped by dominant interests. Those facing societal or structural inequities and a corresponding lack of resources might also be reluctant to propose out-of-the box or experimental solutions due to fear of failing or losing money.
Resisting Futures: What’s Stopping Us?

children.text: Resisting Futures: What’s Stopping Us?

body.text: Stuck in Overdrive

Overwhelm also feeds resistance. “You need space to learn,” said Viswanathan, “and we’re in a moment where we’re being inundated and flooded with too much as humans on the planet right now… and there’s something about [futuring] that requires such a degree of higher order thinking, you need to be in a relaxed state of consciousness to really be able to absorb it.”

The pressure to solve what can seem like insurmountable social issues is coupled with a crippling urgency. “We are a right-now society. Strategic foresight is not a right-now discipline. It’s a long-term planning discipline,” observed Burse. “I feel like there is this pressure for big, transformative, and quick,” said Cruise. But “getting people to imagine and having the time to imagine is hard and slow… I think the difficulty is that the resourcing of the small scale in the slow isn’t always there. Our capital does not support that.”

“Stop loving the problem is a core tenet of the ways that we do our work,” said Harris. “I think it’s especially important in the social sector that this constant talking about all the things that are broken takes people away from actually creating a better future because it sucks all the energy out of the room.”

As a result, those who work in the social change space often find themselves in a “reactive posture where it’s as if the future is just sort of happening to us and we have no agency or voice,” said Viswanathan, “and the future will just be a mirror or a reflection of the trends and the tendencies that are going on right now.”
Our responsibility as futurists is to create ideal visions of futures but in ways that don’t disrupt other futures… complexity is where the excitement comes in.
Futuring doesn’t have to be for everyone

Ultimately, while some find foresight or speculative visioning practices exhilarating, others may find that they clash with their own religious or moral beliefs or the role they choose to play in advancing social change. This is one reason why holding complexity and nuance in such discussions, and anticipating discomfort is key for this work.
Our responsibility as futurists is to help communities create
Resisting Futures: What’s Stopping Us?
ideal visions of the future but in ways that don’t disrupt other communities’ ideal futures… complexity is where the excitement comes in.

Lord offered, “My bias is that I assume that everyone has an imagination… that everyone wants to create and to think of new things and so forth. What I have to step back and remind myself is that some people are very happy just taking the world at face value. They don’t want the work of: ‘Oh, I have to think about what it would be like if it were something else.’ I see the word ‘imagination,’ and I’m just like, oh, this is fun. This is fantastic. I want to be part of this.

But I acknowledge that: Okay, for some people, they’re feeling dread. They’re feeling like those school projects where they had to come up with something that was totally new and they didn’t have that capacity, and they just want someone to tell them what the thing is, tell them what to do, tell ‘em what the steps are. Don’t ask me to create something out of nothing.”
“Bridges built around common interests or goals can support processes to contemplate possible futures together.”

“The balance that you want in any society is the people who open, and complicate, and push, and the people who are traditional and hold the ground in some way,” observed Viswanathan. ”Not everyone will want, or feel safe in, the same future. This comes with the territory, and has to be acknowledged. Bridges built around common interests or goals can support processes to contemplate possible futures together.”
Creating safe environments for futuring

Interviewees have found that when they set the appropriate context, provide enough time and support, and carefully craft invitations for participants to contribute their expertise, experiences, and aspirations to the process, those who engage in futuring work find it energizing, revelatory, and freeing.

To set such a context, interviewees suggest, it’s important to connect how emerging trends and imagined futures relate to the organizer’s and participants’ current challenges and strategies, and to acknowledge that some futures might be stressful to contemplate. Sessions should also involve people who are directly affected by the issue in question. All participants need to feel they belong and bring unique
Resisting Futures: What’s Stopping Us?

children.text: Resisting Futures: What’s Stopping Us?

body.text: knowledge as valuable contributors to the process.

Sometimes despite best efforts, Sinclair noted, structural issues and trauma can make it hard for some to participate, or for participants to hear each other, “because of that level of mistrust.” Facilitators need to ensure that participants feel safe and supported, reflect on and be critical of power imbalances and harms that require repairing, and work to build trust. Approaching future thinking and speculative visioning with “a deep curiosity,” is important, said Viswanathan, as is making participants feel as though they can bring their full selves to such conversations.
Resisting Futures: What’s Stopping Us?
I envision a future where marginalized communities are at the forefront of technological and social innovation.

In Oakland 2045, youth who participated in our program are now community leaders, artists, and technologists. They’ve created a city where equity isn’t just an aspiration but a reality. Imagine walking through Oakland and seeing public art installations that serve both as cultural touchstones and as technological interfaces powered by Ancestral Intelligence. People can communicate with their ancestors through wearable technology, drawing wisdom from the past to navigate their futures. Schools integrate Afrofuturism, Indigenous, and Queer futurisms into their curriculum, and community-based foresight tools are used in policymaking, ensuring that the voices of the historically marginalized are central to decision-making.
Making an Impact: Anecdotes Aha’s
While we may hesitate, not knowing many things, it is the unknown that holds the most powerful possibilities. So take a breath. Take a step.
Norma Wong, When No Thing Works

children.text: Making an Impact: Anecdotes and Aha’s

body.text: A key theme that threaded through our discussions with interviewees was: We know futuring makes a difference, but how do you tell that story?

children.text: Respondents reported that people who participate in activities to imagine better futures expressed a strong desire for more. They described the hunger people had for additional

body.text: engagement with futuring, especially in extremely difficult contexts or moments of crisis. They took pride in their visions.
Some said they experienced a mental shift that they could not un-see or un-know once they had the appropriate space and opportunity to engage their imagination and consider transformative change.

There are many questions about how best to evaluate both the short-term and long-term outcomes of this type of work. But the anecdotal nature of the evidence gathered from these varied projects, and what we heard from respondents, lifts up both a desire for more rigorous and standardized methods and the need to value a more human-centered mode of defining impact. Efforts to evaluate foresight and speculative visioning in various ways are already happening in both the futures studies and evaluation fields—for example, see the Summer 2024 special issue of New Directions for Evaluation, on bridging the two fields.

Bickham raised the urgency of this question for the work that he’s doing in Jackson, Mississippi. He noted that evaluation alone is not sufficient, however. Focusing on understanding and refining approaches that are scalable and spreadable is the key to supporting communities in crisis beyond episodic interventions that can’t be replicated.
Burse named “mindset shift and light bulb moments” as beautiful and difficult to quantify outcomes of his work. Such cognitive breakthroughs have the potential to encourage
Making an Impact: Anecdotes and Aha’s widespread adoption of futuring tools and experiences and create a ripple effect in communities across the country.

Shillingford spoke about the complexities of trying to assess the impact of experiential futures work that is built over several encounters. “How do you create these catalytic moments and then follow them with a sustained thing? And what kind of experiences can support people's transformational journey?” At the same time, many interviewees talked about how long it might take for the impact of such work to surface. “Often it's been people encountering us years later and mentioning the experience that they had,” she said.

Lord shared, “What I do is part of a collective and cumulative effect, and I may never be aware of the end results. What I have seen is the effect of a single piece from a writer who is no longer alive to witness the impact of their words, or the cultural power of a movement of several contemporary writers.”

Some outcomes of the work may never be able to be measured, but that doesn’t dissuade interviewees’ aspirations for the possibility of long-term transformation. Burse noted his passion for strategic foresight stems from a hope that “introducing the concepts… could potentially produce generational change.”
As we discussed impact with interviewees, we surfaced various ways in which the work might have impact: on the interviewees themselves, on individuals, on communities, on fields, and on organizing.
“How do you create these catalytic moments and then follow them with a sustained thing?”
On Interviewees

We asked grantees how RWJF’s support for their work, which ranged from project-specific funding to general operating support, helped them with their practice. They reported that the grants allowed them to:

items.text: Open up necessary space and time for hopeful dreaming and experiencing joy as a counterweight to trauma and the doom and gloom of our public discourse
Develop new tools and methodologies for imagining futures and prototyping possible solutions
Engage communities—local, identity-based, professional—

body.text: Making an Impact: Anecdotes and Aha’s

children.text: Making an Impact: Anecdotes and Aha’s

body.text: to imagine collaboratively and build upon their catalytic experiences

items.text: Deploy creative modalities such as discussing pop culture and playing games to help people concretely envision the implications of possible futures
Give people and organizations the chance to better understand the inequities of the present and work to dismantle them
Celebrate those who face bias as powerful sources of innovation and creativity
Bring the tools of futuring to communities who lack access or the resources to experiment
Challenge entrenched power dynamics inside of the healthcare profession through futuring
Connect with like-minded futures thinkers and build the field
Legitimize social innovation as innovation in a larger culture that treats this as the purview of corporations and entrepreneurs
Elevate care as a key value in foresight and speculative visioning

body.text: On Individuals

Although futuring work is often collective, many of the impacts happen at an individual level. Lord shared that participants can leave her workshops with “a profound adjustment to their worldview or philosophy,” awe, and, what is jokingly called in speculative fiction, a “sens-a-wunda.” She shared: “That brush with the numinous can be an unexpected spark for new creative pathways to a solution, or new ways of seeing and being.”

Mossbridge’s work demonstrated the healing power of people forming a relationship with their past and future selves: “What that taught me is that simply realizing that you exist through time is really the first step to wellbeing,” she said. Incarcerated men in Cook County Jail who participated in her team’s six-week Expanded Human Potential pilot, which included recording messages to their past and future selves, found it so beneficial they asked for it to be extended. “It made them feel like they were real,” she said.
Making an Impact: Anecdotes and Aha’s

Mossbridge explained that for people who “are always getting reinforced that you exist and you’re important, etc., your continuity of yourself is in the water. But if you’re raised in or surrounded by a situation where you’re not sure you’re going to exist tomorrow, you’re also not sure you existed yesterday. You feel very transient. And so, the powerful experience was largely one of: I exist, I'm going to exist tomorrow.”

Proof of the power of futuring skills, tools, and learning is in people’s application of them to real-life situations, and long after they first learn them. Burse observed, “I get pure joy by seeing returning students start implementing those concepts when it comes to their decision-making process once they are done with our curriculum.” These skills also help people adapt and move out of reactive stances in their life and work. People Burse works with “are often in ‘reaction’ mode instead of ‘proaction’ mode. Strategic foresight gives students, community leaders, and executives the insight and techniques to design and create an ambitious, relevant, and compelling future.”

“One of the most surprising impacts has been the personal transformation of the youth involved in Reimagine Oakland 2045,” shared Brooks. “Many of them have told us that they now see themselves as creators of the future rather than passive recipients of it. One of our students became a teaching artist with the Museum of Children’s Art (MOCHA)… Others became activists at their school to advocate for a change. We saw a marked change in how students collaborate to support their peers in articulating their visions for the future during a MOCHA event showcasing youth leadership in futures thinking. The tools they’ve learned—foresight scanning, forecasting, and worldbuilding—have helped them rethink their roles in the world.”
Many of them have told us that they now see themselves as creators of the future rather than passive recipients of it.

Brooks continued: “Our work centers youth voices from BIPOC and resource-scarce communities, giving them the tools to use art and futures thinking as vehicles for empowerment. Seeing students take ownership of their cultural heritage while creating innovative solutions to issues like migration and trauma has been particularly rewarding.”
Making an Impact: Anecdotes and Aha’s

children.text: Making an Impact: Anecdotes and Aha’s

body.text: “Reimagine Oakland 2045 creates a platform for marginalized youth to imagine themselves as active participants in shaping the future,” Brooks said. Artifacts created by students through their AfroRithms from the Future game and program, include “an ancestral bracelet that you could [use to] speak with your ancestors that would give you wisdom during the day and help heal your trauma, our collective and individual trauma.”
“It makes my heart so tender to imagine all these young children of color being invited to dream and imagine an alternative reality through our work.”

Shillingford talked about the moving ways in which participants respond to the experiences that she co-creates. “There was a really sweet one where in Memphis, we created a world with a sound bath room with hammocks and this kid, he fell asleep,” she said, “and he randomly came up to me. I don't think he even knew that I was one of the designers. And he was like, ‘I fell asleep, and I dreamed the most beautiful dream I've never dreamed before.’ And I was like, 'Oh my gosh, I wouldn't even have written that as an outcome.’”
“Introducing the concepts that could potentially produce generational change is the linchpin. It’s the great equalizer.”

Shillingford and her team have heard from at least 25 people over the last few years who use Intelligent Mischief’s images and provocations in school and university classrooms and at home with their children. These individual-level impacts have potential to catalyze change on a wider scale: “It makes my heart so tender to imagine all these young children of color being invited to dream and imagine an alternative reality through our work.”

Burse shared that, “Breaking the barriers between the known and unknown is one of the most important things to me when it comes to introducing strategic foresight. For far too long, communities of color have lacked support and resources. That often has a short-term impact, but far too often, a long-term impact is overlooked… Introducing the concepts that could potentially produce generational change is the linchpin. It's the great equalizer.”
Making an Impact: Anecdotes and Aha’s
Lau ra

I love imagining a world where social workers will be actively involved in utilizing futures thinking and foresight practice in matters of human development, human wellbeing, and health equity (amid the larger ecosystem of equity issues).

In this scenario, they will emerge from educational programs with an astute, balanced, and imaginative understanding of the ways that the past, present, and future relate to each other. They will be courageous, creative, and strength-based in engaging communities in deeper aspiration and agency about co-creating democratic futures and in claiming space from powerful institutions and actors to assert those ideas. They will ‘roll’ more effectively by anticipating and even engaging in nonlinear change in the world around them and will empower others to understand and do the same. They will celebrate evolution not only in the profession, but in the larger change environment and learn to see and engage with early signals of change that portend positive change, while learning to be more effective activists regarding those that are in violation of key ethical foundations.

children.text: Making an Impact: Anecdotes and Aha’s

body.text: Harris noted her work to strengthen individuals’ “future thinking abilities” was “helping people see the future in a more positive way. For example, through the Field Trips to the Future project, rural leaders learned that the future isn’t something to fear, but something they can shape through their decisions today. This has made them feel more hopeful and empowered to tackle challenges in their communities.”
On Communities

Working with a local community over time can also lead to shifts in behavior. Bickham talked about the impact of introducing futuring knowledge and mindsets across a set of thinkers and organizations in a region, and fostering partnerships at the local, state, and national levels. Acknowledging many colleagues and supporters who have contributed to the work, he noted growing traction and adoption of culturally informed foresight practices in the Jackson Medical Mall Foundation and beyond.

“In most cases, futurism has been an unknown concept in general, and equitably and culturally informed futurism as a methodology in particular was unknown until I introduced it,” he said. “More specifically, the Young Futurist Project, our youth leadership program, was the first of its kind in the state.” He also noted that the creation of Imagine 22, a unit of the Jackson Medical Mall Foundation designed to implement a “future-ready agenda” across the organization, was a new development.

Bickham collaborated with Harris to bring people living in Jackson through the foresight training offered by Harris’ FutureGood Studio. Afterward, Harris visited Jackson for a nonprofit and philanthropy conference and observed, “It was really powerful to see that you created this constellation of people that are thinking about a different future that are then influencing all of these conversations. And they're not the one lone crazy voice in the room that everybody ignores... It's like one would speak up and then somebody else would support, and it just really increased the quality of the conversation, and it made the future something so natural. It was really powerful.”

Harris noted the shift in thinking about the future in more positive ways is spreading across communities. “Leaders are starting new projects, on things like rural economic development through recreation and bridging political divides, that are focused on long-term solutions. It’s also changing how people talk about the future—there’s more excitement and creativity about what’s possible. One surprising result is how quickly this sense of optimism has caught on, encouraging
Making an Impact: Anecdotes and Aha’s 98 people to think bigger and work together to create the future they want.”

In the Community Futures School work, Brooks noted, “On a community level, the project has sparked conversations about how Oakland can evolve in ways that prioritize equity and sustainability. We’ve also seen broader cultural shifts as these youth contribute their voices to local exhibitions and public discourse, making futures thinking more accessible to BIPOC communities.”

Gorbis sees opportunity for impact when IFTF is “able to connect our research, which often involves historical analysis of trends and insights from the data, to communities that have a lived experience of these trends.” She notes working with communities such as Inland Empire, “one of the poorest regions in California, to help them envision building a more equitable economy, with the focus on Black residents” as an important example of this impact. Gorbis highlighted an instance of this resonance at a community convening, where she shared a finding that wealth is no longer created through income, but rather through inheritance and property: “An African-American [community] leader came up to me after my presentation to hug me and said, ‘you have validated what we’ve been feeling.’”

“What I worry about is that the expert views, because they come from elite places, are actually being discounted by the people on the ground who have the lived experience,” Gorbis said. But instead, “when you bring the two together, there's something magical that happens.”

Shillingford explained that the team’s installation of signs in their neighborhood with the provocation “What if BedStuy was a Black Utopia?” led to discussions about the history, present, and future of the area. The project inspired local artists “to engage community members in imagining the future of the neighborhood as a safe and healthy place for Black people.”
...the provocation “What if BedStuy was a Black Utopia?” led to discussions about the history, present, and future of the area.

She also shared that their work has been shifting people’s orientation to certain community spaces: A participant at a Carnegie Hall exhibition during a 2022 Afrofuturism festival said that they never thought they would see their culture
Making an Impact: Anecdotes and Aha’s 99

children.text: Making an Impact: Anecdotes and Aha’s

body.text: represented in that space.

Burse observed that "for students, community leaders, and executives, most have taken a history class, but not a future class.” But he noted that governments and companies such as Disney or Deloitte use foresight. “These large institutions that can fund it understand the importance and significance of what the trends are telling them for the next 15 years, because they can start tailoring their business strategies,” he said. “So that’s [the] same approach we need to take with our different communities and stakeholders. If we can fund it and find a sustainable method to do that, what kind of impacts can we start having in the next 15 years?”
On Fields and Movements

Foresight and speculative visioning can be a powerful tool for entire fields or professions. For example, in 2019, RWJF funded Amy Webb of Future Today Strategy Group to conduct a workshop with leaders in the nursing profession. They identified trends that might impact healthcare from 2020–2030 and developed scenarios for operating in those potential futures.

Nissen’s Social Work Futures Lab is another example of bringing this work into fields where it had previously been unknown. She explained that “a literal ‘new space’ has been constructed for the profession of social work” in which scholars and practitioners are applying futuring approaches “to real-world social work challenges.”

This new lens is having a ripple effect throughout the social work profession, with national social work leadership organizations and educational associations exploring their capacity to incorporate futures thinking, as well as education programs adding foresight practice into their offerings. Nissen noted that “foresight has the potential to change social work in positive ways” and, in turn, “social work has the potential to change foresight. This is particularly true in the insertion of some key value propositions including justice, equity, wellbeing, and power analysis in any and all matters of the future.”

IFTF has had a significant impact on many professional spaces, including community groups, business, and government, having trained thousands of people in foresight techniques and offering courses, newsletters, and influential books by affiliates that have been inspired by their Equitable Enterprise work. “We believe that futures thinking in itself is a transformational practice for creating better futures
Making an Impact: Anecdotes and Aha’s 100 for civil society,” Gorbis shared.

In addition to some of her students describing themselves as futurists after her training, Harris noted the emergence of a “core group of rural champions who now inspire and galvanize others to incorporate a future orientation into rural strategies and actions.” She observed that “seeing participants collaborate and share innovative practices from rural areas was particularly rewarding, as these examples now serve as signals of potential pathways forward for others in the field.”

For brown, “To be a futurist means to really be a now-ist… Any organization that's trying to change the world is actually a futurist organization… Some people shy away from it as if it's escapist or dreamy or something, but really we're trying to create a world that we've never lived in… all of it is futurist behavior to me.”
To be a futurist means to really be a now-ist… Any organization that's trying to change the world is actually a futurist organization...

Bringing the tools of futuring to not just nonprofits but movements can help them better anticipate and plan. “It makes me think about a kind of power and stability that comes from having the strategy [informed by] foresight or even visioning,” said Shillingford. “It would almost be speculative movementbuilding, speculative organizing… I actually crave some of that for the movement sector, which can be a little bit reactionary without an institution that is dedicated towards doing that foresight work within that ecosystem.” She described social movement leaders using Intelligent Mischief’s World Building questions to facilitate gatherings with their teams and grantees to consider “bold and beautiful ideas of what the future could be.”

“When we land policy without having first shifted culture, it’s almost like the practice falls apart... and we end up fighting for the crumbs of that vision we once had,” said brown. That is why her writing and editing work focuses on advancing both narrative and social transformation. She cited her influential partnership with AK Press in publishing the Emergent Strategy series, which features her bestselling books alongside those of peers whose ideas she champions. The series includes a genre-bending mix of analysis, personal reflection, poetry, and speculative fiction exploring alternate worlds and new
Making an Impact: Anecdotes and Aha’s

children.text: Making an Impact: Anecdotes and Aha’s

body.text: paradigms for social change.

Ogbunu’s approach also highlights how futuring can also be a vital bridge across fields: “I have enjoyed combining varied approaches, perspectives, and methods to ask questions about the world we are in,” he shared. “I especially enjoy how this approach requires one to challenge oneself, to interact with experts from fields that one might not have been historically embedded in. I truly believe the next generation of big discoveries relies on this sort of intellectual agility.”
Making an Impact: Anecdotes and Aha’s
Tris ta
The future I hope we created through the Field Trips to the Future project is one where rural communities and Native nations across the U.S. are thriving, innovative, and inclusive.

Picture rural leaders using future-thinking tools to shape their communities so that everyone feels like they belong, lives with dignity, and has opportunities to succeed: Local businesses are thriving, young people are excited to stay or return to their hometowns, and communities are leading the way with creative solutions in areas like sustainable farming and clean energy. I imagine rural leaders becoming both visionaries and problem-solvers, using data and new ideas to address challenges like climate change and economic inequality. These communities will no longer be seen as behind the times, but as forward-thinking places that others look to for inspiration. They will be recognized for their innovative approaches to creating fair, sustainable, and vibrant futures for everyone who lives there.

children.text: Building the Field: Insights Opportunities

body.text: Remember to imagine and craft the worlds you cannot live without, just as you dismantle the ones you cannot live within.
Ruha Benjamin, Imagination:A Manifesto

children.text: Building the Field: Insights and Opportunities

body.text: How can the field of philanthropy seize the opportunities of foresight and futuring in these times, moving forward with accountability to support true justice and transformation?

children.text: Interviewees offered numerous insights into the practice of democratizing the imagination of the future, observations about how the field is evolving, and suggestions for how to strengthen it.

body.text: To strengthen the practice of democratizing the imagination of the future, interviewees suggested:

Host mindfully: Interviewees advocated for gathering communities to consider futures in ways that are celebratory, generative, safe, and supportive. This includes intentionally building care into the convening on multiple levels, holding space for people’s grief through transitions and challenging times, designing trauma-informed spaces, encouraging transdisciplinary collaboration and deep relationship building, choosing face-to-face interactions where possible and appropriate from an accessibility perspective, and facilitating space where people are allowed to be vulnerable and their full selves.

Costanza-Chock shared, “How do you make sure that the space is truly accessible?… So everything from making sure that people's childcare needs are met to that the space is physically accessible, making sure that you're really building in a lot of breaks and moments when people can relax and eat food and be in their bodies, and basically all the stuff that needs to happen to make it so that people can really show up and do that imagination work together.”
Convene for creative sparks: “I think one of the most powerful things to push imagination is to convene smart people with loose connections,” said Gómez-Márquez.
Building the Field: Insights and Opportunities 108

“That's where I've seen the sparks… because then it can generate just big ideas.” Young encouraged considering the conditions that enable people to imagine and prototype, thinking about the types of information, experiences, permission, workplace cultures, or spaces that people might find supportive in order to participate in meaningful ways.

Mossbridge noted the importance of engaging people in spaces that encourage a measure of uncertainty: “The unknowing is where you get the imagination... if you can help people get a little unsettled, again, a little bit of where that they don't really know, then that's where imagination comes in… that's like social acupuncture.”
I think one of the most powerful things to push imagination is to convene smart people with loose connections.

Fund practice-building: Interviewees called for supporting and scaling up skill-building and training for the next generation of futurists to meet the needs of our time. “Fund the risk takers at whatever level you find them,” said Bickham. This includes intentionally convening and resourcing specific cohorts such as Black and other futurists of color, grassroots practitioners, and those seeking interdisciplinary spaces. “I would love to see what it would look like to start a minoritybased Association of Professional Futurists type organization,” said Creston Burse. “We have to create an ecosystem and an environment where people feel safe and they feel supported and they have the ability to be exposed to these different things.”

Ogbunu recommended supporting nontraditional futurist translators and ambassadors outside of the ivory tower— what he calls people who are "delightfully interesting and edgy, people who are being productive, who are sharing their work and are giving us unusual ideas.”
“... if you can help people get a little unsettled, again, a little bit of where that they don’t really know, then that’s where imagination comes in… that’s like social acupuncture.”
Building the Field: Insights and Opportunities 109

children.text: Building the Field: Insights and Opportunities

body.text: Build capacity of futuring practitioners: Interviewees identified a need to shore up resources to grow their capacity for futuring and impact. This includes funds directed toward evolving existing initiatives, promoting generative collaboration among futuring practitioners, business model and development planning support to help practitioners grow into their niches, expanding training in futuring skills and access to tools, and hosting digital tools to support mutual resource sharing. New models for sustaining foresight and visioning practice are also needed—whether the work is done in a for-profit or nonprofit context, or through experiments in hybrid models such as networks or social impact businesses.
“Fund the risk takers at whatever level you find them,” said Bickham.

“I'm hearing from a ton of nonprofits that are like, ‘Yes, I want to do this [training], and we don't have the financial resources to do it,’” said Harris. “So we have been trying to figure out both how to subsidize spots, which we've mostly been doing out of business profits, and we've been giving away a lot of seats, especially to people that run organizations [focused on people of color]. And it's not sustainable, but it's really important.”

Provide resources to move beyond visioning: Interviewees noted that the work of democratizing the imagination of the future is only the first step in building more equitable and just futures. Once new possibilities, processes, or solutions are imagined, support is needed for research and development so as not to leave participants in such processes hanging, and to test the viability of the ideas they have generated together.

Gómez-Márquez noted, “My problem with some of the design future stuff is that it's great to put a crazy idea out there, but if you're doing it just to provoke, I think often that can just be that... just a provocateur thing.” Sinclair noted a heartbreaking moment where she met a participant in a project about the future of Lagos, Nigeria, that she thought looked amazing from afar. “I was like: ‘Oh my God, you're part of that thing. It was so awesome!’ He responded, ‘It was incredibly awesome, but I almost wish it had never happened… because we dreamed up all of these incredible ideas, but there were no resources to make them a reality.”

Support retreat and reflection: Some interviewees described the futuring and foresight field as a largely siloed, competitive, professional sphere that is unknown or misunderstood by the vast majority of the population. This

Building the Field: Insights and Opportunities 110 has meant that they have had to use a “push strategy” to engage communities to participate and funders to allocate funding to infrastructure and resources, as well as do a lot of heavy lifting to educate and translate the language of foresight into appropriate experiences for the average person. These constraints drive futures thinkers to navigate many interprofessional contexts—work that can take a significant toll on their wellbeing.

“If the philanthropists could ask us what it is that we need to do to flourish—not what it is that the communities need to flourish, but what it is that we as futurists and activists and visionaries need, I think that's going to be the most redemptive thing,” said Bickham. “Because we give up our whole selves.”

Bring in different generations: Interviewees including Brooks, Burse, Cruise, and Shillingford advocated for engaging youth in foresight and speculative visioning. One suggestion involved translating specialized futures-thinking jargon to be more accessible to many more people, especially youth.

If the philanthropists could ask us what it is that we need to do to flourish—what it is that we as futurists and activists and visionaries need, I think that’s going to be the most redemptive thing. Because we give up our whole selves.

But inviting participants from across the age spectrum can provide benefits as well. Futuring “works really well when you have young people and you have elderly folks that are participating because elderly folks can see patterns that have happened over time and bring insight into ‘Here's what I think this next wave is going to look like,’” said Harris.

“I do think that imagining the future should be a crossgenerational project,” said Lord. "There will definitely be a situation where you might find the young want this and the old want that. However… people who have ties to the past, who know what it was like to say, live through World War II, live through the Cold War, and actually be worried about being bombed out of existence into a nuclear winter [are] going to bring that lived experience into their imagining what the future should look like, and also the caution of knowing the dreams or the utopias that they had back then that got smashed in particular ways.”
Building the Field: Insights and Opportunities

children.text: Building the Field: Insights and Opportunities

body.text: Beware of unintended harms: An important aspect of ethical and critical futuring practice is to consider and work to avoid possible unintended downsides of the work—especially in an increasingly polarized society. Making space for imagining a more equitable and diverse array of possible futures can come with risks. Possible concerns raised by some interviewees included ways in which:

items.text: Cultural or revolutionary language and concepts can become diluted and lose their original edge
Extractive corporate or political systems can co-opt speculative futuring ideas and turn what began as cautionary tales into harmful products or policies
Inviting people to co-create stories about the future that shine a critical light on the present can be seen as a threat to systems or ideologies, and draw hostile responses
Inviting visioning without resources to make progress toward those visions can cause mistrust and frustration

body.text: Other specific tactics suggested by interviewees to guide futures field-building efforts included:

items.text: Investing in libraries as spaces for collective imagination and community engagement
Investing in the creation of science fiction from diverse communities
Engaging and supporting Black women in futuring
Combining foresight and speculative visioning approaches to placemaking, drawing community members in to reimagine their own neighborhoods and states

body.text: Building the Field: Insights and Opportunities
adri enne
I’ve started to vision mostly in terms of how we are with each other, what we practice, and how we are. What I feel like we’re doing is we’re in relationship to a place that we live.

We feel secure, we feel belonging. Everyone feels a fundamental sense of belonging. Children feel a fundamental sense of safety. They are taken care of by anyone who’s in their community. And if we’re doing organizing work, the work that we’re doing is mostly governance and facilitation and mediation. So we’re not in a constant state of survivalbased crisis. We’re in a sense of managing relationship to a place that wants to provide us with abundant resources, and we are receiving those resources and making good lives from it.
What We Learned, What Comes Next
Imagination is a futures tool, a human need, but also a present action.
Ytasha Womack, The Afrofuturist Evolution

children.text: What We Learned, and What Comes Next

body.text: Democratizing the imagination of the future has never been more necessary.

As the systems that produce health collapse around us, many are seeking visions of a new media system, a new public health system, a new health science knowledge system. These new visions must be shaped by the brilliance of many—not just a powerful few.

Over the past seven years, we have learned so much from the futuring practitioners RWJF has supported—each trying to sense, imagine, and design more equitable tomorrows in dialogue with the communities they serve. These insights have bolstered our belief that when more people have the time and resources to imagine, we unlock the collective wisdom needed to create a healthier world.

By sharing what we and our grantees have learned in this report, our goal has been to show leaders, practitioners, organizers, and funders how the practice of futuring can help them anticipate the impact of emerging trends, build resilience through co-creation and creativity, conceive of the futures they want to inhabit, and discover new ways to work with others to bring those futures to life. We wanted to inspire more people across the social sector to spend time in the future.
We hope we have achieved this, and that you have arrived here ready to jump deeper into futuring. We hope you are asking: How do I turn this inspiration into action?
As artists, advocates, activists, and community visionaries, how do we take that next or first step on our journeys to imagining—and then creating—better futures?

As health equity and racial justice leaders and funders, how do we resource our communities in ways that provide them with the time, space, and expertise to imagine futures beyond their wildest dreams? Here are some possible answers:
What We Learned, and What Comes Next
1. Interrogate the assumptions you hold about ”the future” and what is possible.

children.text: The future is not guaranteed. It does not come with any sort of semblance of automatic progress, and it’s something that needs to be thought about critically.”—Brandon Ogbunu

body.text: 2. Embrace imagination as a liberatory practice. Shed oppressive systems, power structures, and dominant narratives as you dream more deeply about an antiracist future.

children.text: If you hold onto old systems as they collapse, you collapse with them.”—Trista Harris

body.text: 3. Elevate joy, play, and creativity in your futuring. Find hope even as you confront injustice today and prepare for deeply challenging tomorrows.

children.text: When I get in these spaces [marginalized communities] and I have the fortitude and the strength and the joy, rather than feeling dilapidated, people just bloom. They quantum leap in their imaginations.”—David Bickham

body.text: What We Learned, and What Comes Next

children.text: What We Learned, and What Comes Next

body.text: 4. Seek out futuring practitioners whose personal experiences, cultural insight, and strong connections with communities make them more adept at illuminating unexpected paths forward as they spot signals, patterns, and trends that others miss.

children.text: Historically, the most marginalized communities, their visions of the future, because it implied their own freedom, actually has brought about the biggest transformations in society for the better of all.”—Aisha Shillingford

body.text: 5. Provide support to the many people who are democratizing the imagination of the future, helping them become more visible and more connected to one another, and enabling this growing field to flourish.

children.text: The future isn’t one big grant. It’s little tiny decisions made by millions of people every day.”—Kamal Sinclair

body.text: From our perspective, there is much more to explore together and collectively learn about how democratizing the imagination of the future can help us all get to tomorrows where health is no longer a privilege, but a right. We see this as the start of an ongoing conversation—one RWJF plans to continue.
The future isn’t fixed. And that changes everything.
What We Learned, and What Comes Next
GLOSSARY

Engaging in the futuring space exposes us to many approaches, movements, tools, and perspectives. Below are brief descriptions of some of the terms and phrases that appear in this report. We hope this list serves as a starting point to pique your curiosity.

Afrofuturism: A cultural and aesthetic movement blending imagination, technology, critical theory, and Black perspectives to envision liberated futures. Embracing science fiction and centering African diaspora culture and experiences, it can be engaged as a framework to rewrite history, challenge oppression, and contemplate possibilities beyond present conditions. [Sources: Womack, Brooks & Anderson, Brandon]
Anticipation: The sensing or expectation of future events, sometimes paired with preparation. [Sources: Copenhagen Institute for Futures Studies, Bourgeois]

Artifact: A tangible or conceptual representation (such as an object, text, or piece of art) of an imagined future, created to make that potential world more concrete, experiential, and accessible for reflection and discussion. [Sources: Institute for the Future, Candy & Potter, Candy & Dunagan]

Crip/Disabled Futurisms: A movement that envisions futures centering disabled people, knowledge, and ways of living. It works to advance disability justice and counter ableism in the present and future. [Sources: Piepzna-Samarasinha, Neilson, Grist, Griffith, Society of Disabled Oracles]

Critical Futures: Approaches to futures practices that critique power, question the status quo, and challenge dominant frameworks and systems to work toward just and preferred futures. Critical Futures stand in contrast to predictive methods and approaches that focus on shaping a singular vision of “the future.” [Sources: Gidley, Slaughter, Inayatullah, Gidley]

Decolonizing Futures: Approaches to futures practices that seek to dismantle the coloniality of foresight, interrogate visions of the future dominated by eurocentric and western perspectives, challenge assumptions and power that currently shape society, and go beyond inclusion to center a vast array of non-western perspectives, methods, and knowledge. [Sources: Krishnan, Bisht, Gesturing Towards Decolonial Futures]

Democratizing Futures: Expanding access to fair and active participation in imagining and shaping futures, as well as going beyond inclusion to consider how power dynamics and systemic inequities impact participation. [Sources: Sitra, Barendregt et al]

Design Futures: Where design meets futures thinking, often using creative, speculative, and experiential methods to prototype and shape scenarios and possible solutions for desired futures. [Sources: Candy & Dunagan, Angheloiu et al., Kelliher et al.]
Futures: Emphasizing the plurality of possible futures, rejecting the singular “future” driven by the status quo in favor of diverse visions and alternative futures. [Source: Gidley]

Futuring: “Applied future exploration and action,” ranging from anticipation of perceived threats and opportunities, to envisioning how trends might shape next developments, to discovery of surprises or openings, to shaping of possible futures through “simulated experiences … via prototypes, media, artefacts, or immersion, to test-drive new realities.” [Source: Smith & Ashby]
Futuremaking: The act of exploring and negotiating a range of possible futures, and engaging in work to bring preferred futures into being. [Source: SSIR]

Futures Literacy: The ability to engage one’s imagination in various contexts to navigate and shape the future, understand why it is important to do so, and recognize underlying assumptions that might be involved. [Sources: Miller, Copenhagen Institute for Futures Studies]

Futures Studies: The field of study dedicated to exploring and understanding the range of potential futures (possible, probable, preferable) and the perspectives, values, and assumptions that drive them respectively. [Sources: Inayatullah, Copenhagen Institute for Futures Studies]

Futures Thinking: An approach to strategically explore the range of possible futures using diverse tools, inform decisionmaking, and build capacity to foster preferable futures and transformative change in the present. [Sources: Inayatullah, Canina et al., IFTF]
Imagination: The ability to conceive of new possibilities, crucial for shaping futures and navigating uncertainty.

Indigenous Futurisms: A movement that engages Indigenous traditional and ancestral knowledge, technologies, and culture to remix and expand past, present, and futures centering Indigenous people, narratives, and ways of being. Through a variety of different modalities, such as speculative fiction and art, it involves the interrogation of and recovery from impacts of colonization and oppressive systems to revitalize Indigenous culture and envision decolonial futures. [Sources: Dillon, Furtado et al., Kwaymullina, Killick]

Liberatory Futures: Refers to futures where interlocking systems of white supremacy, colonialism, and other oppressions are dismantled, and where the freedom and thriving of people most impacted by these systems are centered and affirmed. Liberatory futures are enacted through enduring imagination and action to resist, abolish, and transform harms that these systems cause. [Sources: Nassiri-Ansari et al., Wilson, Gumbs, O’Brien & Simon, Shillingford, Takla, Priyadharshini]

Participatory Futures: Approaches to futures practices that expand participation in imagining and changing possible futures to the public, rather than limiting to policymakers and professionals. Intentionally including alternative points of view in envisioning and shaping future possibilities. [Sources: Nesta, Krishnan, Barendregt et al.]
Prototyping: Experimenting with, creating, and refining tangible ideas, products, and tools for a variety of future scenarios. [Sources: SOIF/California 100, Miller]

Radical Imagination: The ability to conceive of new possibilities outside of the constraints and structures of the present, and applying this to challenge oppressive systems and envision just, transformed futures. [Sources: Hill-Jarrett, Haiven & Khasnabish, Everyday Activism Network]

Queer Futurism: A framework for imagining futures that center LGBTQ2SIA+ culture, lives, and experiences, expanding possibilities for change beyond current conceptions of identity, and challenging dominant narratives through a queer lens. [Sources: de Paula, Larsen, Grist, Muñoz, Brooks]

Sankofa: A framework that derives from a word in Twi, which means to “go back and gather.” It entails rooting futures thinking in history and ancestry to create more informed visions of tomorrow. [Source: Preston]
Scenario: A story of how the future might occur based on key drivers, trends, and assumptions. [Sources: Copenhagen Institute for Futures Studies, Bourgeois, Amer et al.]

Scenario Planning: Developing and presenting multiple stories about potential futures to inform strategic decisions and provoke critical consideration of alternative perspectives and possibilities. [Source: Amer et al.]

Signal: Emerging information, such as headlines, art, scientific innovations, or social behaviors, that is perceived and interpreted to consider its potential impact on future developments. Signals may vary in strength and are easier to interpret as more is known about them. [Sources: van Veen & Ortt, Gorbis, IFTF]
Speculation: Considering and imagining aspects of otherworldly or possible futures using a range of knowledges. [Sources: Hovorka & Mueller]

Speculative Fiction: An expansive and diverse genre of fiction that explores alternative and nonrealist ways of being, provokes thought on possible futures, may include postcolonial perspectives and critiques of western values, and encapsulates a wide range of narrative fiction, like dystopias and utopias. [Sources: Gill, Oziewicz]

Speculative Visioning: Creating imaginative futures to challenge assumptions and provoke new possibilities for action in the present. Includes an array of practices across several creative disciplines and encouraging participants to explore a range of futures in which we can all thrive. Modalities may include science fiction, design fiction to create futures artifacts, art, game and experiential design, and more. [Sources: This is a term that is not in wide use across the field, but that we are introducing here to call out the key role that imagination plays in inspiring people to take action toward better futures.]

Strategic Foresight: A discipline that applies a structured approach to anticipating possible future developments and planning for them. [Sources: Copenhagen Institute for Futures Studies, SOIF/California 100]
Trend: A general pattern of change over time; megatrends are large-scale, long-term shifts. [Sources: SOIF/California 100, Copenhagen Institute for Futures Studies; Cranfield University]
Worldbuilding: Crafting fictional worlds with detailed histories, cultures, and social structures. [Sources: Zaidi, Stackelberg & McDowell, Shillingford]
